stem: Which of these passwords is the hardest to guess?

choices.text: Password
Qwerty
Michael2024
9!kQz$7wLp2@

explanation: Long, random, and mixing uppercase letters, digits, and symbols: far more resistant than a common word or a first name.

stem: A friend sends you a strange message containing a link, with no explanation at all. What is the right reaction?

choices.text: Enter your credentials on the page that opens
Share the link with other contacts
Click the link right away
Ask them through another channel whether they really sent it

explanation: A hacked account can send out links: checking with the sender keeps you out of the trap.

stem: You receive a text message: "Your parcel is on hold, pay $2 here: [link]". What is this most likely to be?

choices.text: A normal message from your mail carrier
A special offer from the delivery company
A scam attempt (phishing) to steal your money
An update for your phone

explanation: These fake parcel texts are designed to steal your banking details: do not reply to them.

stem: Why is it recommended to install your phone's updates?

choices.text: To disable the camera
To delete all your photos
Because they often fix security flaws
To change the screen color

explanation: Updates close security holes that attackers could otherwise exploit.

stem: What does two-factor authentication (a code on top of your password) give you?

choices.text: Automatic deletion of spam
Extra protection if your password is stolen
A shorter password to remember
A faster Internet connection

explanation: Even with your password, an attacker cannot log in without the second code.

stem: An unknown website asks for your email password to "verify your identity". What do you do?

choices.text: You refuse: no legitimate site ever asks for that
You give a similar but slightly different password
You hand it over to save time
You type it in uppercase in the form

explanation: No legitimate service ever asks for the password to another account.

stem: Which sign should make you suspect a phishing email?

choices.text: It shows today's date
It is written in your own language
It contains mistakes and creates a strong sense of urgency
It offers an unsubscribe link

explanation: Spelling mistakes and manufactured urgency are classic phishing red flags.

stem: What is a password manager for?

choices.text: Translating web pages
Remembering and generating strong passwords for you
Cleaning your phone screen
Speeding up video games

explanation: It securely stores unique, strong passwords so you never have to memorize them.

stem: Posting a photo of your boarding pass or your bank card online is…

choices.text: Completely risk-free
Recommended as proof of your trip
A good way to gain followers
Risky, because that sensitive information can be abused

explanation: These documents contain personal data that fraudsters can reuse.

stem: Which unlock code is the least secure for a phone?

choices.text: A long, complicated pattern
A six-digit code with varied digits
A fingerprint

explanation: A code as simple as 0000 can be guessed almost instantly.

stem: Where is it best to download an app to your phone from?

choices.text: From a link received by text message
From the official store (App Store, Google Play)
From an unknown site found at random
From a USB stick found in the street

explanation: Official stores vet the apps they distribute, which limits the risk.

stem: An email saying "Your bank: click quickly to unlock your account" contains a link. What is the right reaction?

choices.text: Forward the email to your contacts
Click the link to check
Do not click and contact the bank through an official channel
Reply with your credentials

explanation: Urgency plus a link to click is a classic phishing signal. Always verify through an official channel.

stem: A caller pressures you to pay immediately or face penalties. What is the right reaction?

choices.text: Give your card number over the phone
Pay right away to avoid trouble
Call back the displayed number without checking it
Stay calm, hang up, and verify through an official channel

explanation: Urgency is a manipulation technique: take the time to verify.

stem: On a public computer (at a library), what should you do after checking your email?

choices.text: Write your password on a note next to the screen
Log out and close the browser
Save the password in the browser
Leave the session open for next time

explanation: Logging out prevents the next person from getting into your account.

stem: An email from your "bank" asks you to confirm your PIN and your card number. What should you make of it?

choices.text: It is a normal security procedure
It is mandatory once a year
It is a way to earn loyalty points
It is fraud: a bank never asks for that by email

explanation: A bank will never ask for your PIN or your card number by email.

stem: Your important photos exist only on your phone. What should you do so you don't lose them?

choices.text: Send them to a stranger
Lower the screen brightness
Keep a copy in the cloud or on a computer
Nothing, they are already safe

explanation: A second copy protects your memories if the phone is lost or breaks.

stem: An email looks like it comes from a well-known service, but the sender address is odd (e.g. service@scam-xyz.info). What do you do?

choices.text: You reply with your password
You trust the logo displayed
You click every link to check
You treat it as suspicious: the sender address doesn't match

explanation: A sender address that doesn't add up is often the giveaway of an impersonation attempt.

stem: Your best friend asks you for the password to your online account. What is the best attitude?

choices.text: Share it, they can be trusted
Send it to them by text message
Keep your password to yourself, even with people close to you
Write it on a shared sticky note

explanation: A password stays personal: the more it circulates, the more likely it is to leak.

stem: An open Wi-Fi network named "Free_Fast_WiFi" has no password at all. What do you do?

choices.text: You connect to it and pay for your online purchases
You share the network with your whole family
You type all your passwords on it
You stay cautious and avoid any sensitive activity

explanation: Open Wi-Fi can be monitored: avoid sending sensitive data over it.

stem: What does the "Find My Device" feature allow when it has been turned on ahead of time?

choices.text: Charging the battery faster
Sending free text messages
Locating or remotely locking a lost phone
Improving call quality

explanation: This feature helps you locate, lock, or wipe a device you've misplaced.

stem: A message announces that you've won an iPhone in a lottery you never entered. What is it?

choices.text: An opportunity to grab very quickly
An official gift from the manufacturer
A tax refund
Almost certainly a scam

explanation: Winning a prize you never entered for is a classic scam lure.

stem: What is the worst way to keep your passwords?

choices.text: On a sticky note stuck to the computer screen
In your head, without writing them down
In a notebook kept in a locked drawer
In a password manager

explanation: A password on display for everyone to see is extremely easy to steal.

stem: Which of these habits makes a password stronger?

choices.text: Using a long passphrase made of several words
Typing 1234 to go faster
Simply choosing the word "password"
Using your first name followed by your year of birth

explanation: A long, varied passphrase is far harder to guess than a simple word or an obvious sequence.

stem: On the phone, a stranger claims to be a "technician" and asks to take remote control of your computer. What do you do?

choices.text: You refuse and hang up
You pay for the "repair" by card
You agree and install the software they point you to
You give them your credentials

explanation: This fake tech support is trying to take over your device: hang up.

stem: You receive an email from a stranger with an attachment you weren't expecting. What is the right reaction?

choices.text: Don't open it and delete the email
Reply with your mailing address
Forward it to all your contacts
Open it right away out of curiosity

explanation: An unexpected attachment may carry a virus: it's better not to open it.

stem: Why is it useful to lock your phone with a passcode or a fingerprint?

choices.text: To make the battery last longer
To improve photo quality
To stop someone from getting at your data if you lose it
To speed up the Internet connection

explanation: Locking the screen protects your information if the phone is lost or stolen.

stem: Why is it better to avoid using the same password on all your accounts?

choices.text: Because if one site is breached, all your accounts become vulnerable
Because passwords expire faster
Because it slows down your computer
Because it is against the law

explanation: A unique password per account keeps a single leak from compromising everything.

stem: On free public Wi-Fi (a café, a train station), which activity is the riskiest?

choices.text: Checking the weather
Watching a video
Logging in to your online banking
Reading the news

explanation: On a public network, it's best to avoid sensitive activities such as banking.

stem: What is a backup of your files for?

choices.text: Making the phone faster
Blocking ads
Automatically deleting duplicate files
Getting your data back if your device is lost or fails

explanation: A backup lets you recover your data after a loss, a theft, or a hardware failure.

stem: Someone claiming to be your bank asks you for the code you received by text message in order to "validate" something. What do you do?

choices.text: You never share that code, with anyone
You read it out slowly to avoid mistakes
You give the code, since it's the bank
You give only the first few digits

explanation: A real bank never asks for that code: don't share it with anyone.

stem: What is two-factor authentication (2FA / MFA) for?

choices.text: Encrypting the hard drive
Blocking ads
Remembering your password for you
Requiring a second proof of identity on top of the password

explanation: Even if the password leaks, the attacker still lacks the second factor (code, app, or key).

stem: A flashlight app requests access to your contacts. What is the most cautious response?

choices.text: Allow it, it's probably needed for the app to work
Deny it, because that permission has nothing to do with the app's function
Restart the phone to make the prompt go away
Share the list manually by email

explanation: A permission unrelated to the app's purpose should be denied on principle.

stem: What does regularly reviewing the permissions granted to mobile apps let you do?

choices.text: Increase screen brightness
Permanently disable notifications
Remove unnecessary access to location, microphone, or photos
Turn the device into a Wi-Fi hotspot

explanation: Reviewing permissions lets you revoke unnecessary access and limit data collection.

stem: How do regular backups help against ransomware?

choices.text: They stop the ransomware from running
They remove the malware automatically
They let you restore your data without paying the ransom
They alert the police in real time

explanation: Having a clean backup lets you recover your files without giving in to extortion.

stem: Which best practice does the "3-2-1" backup rule describe?

choices.text: Three backups a day, two on weekends, one a month
Three passwords, two accounts, one device
Three copies, on two different media, with one offsite
Three antivirus programs, two firewalls, one VPN

explanation: The 3-2-1 rule calls for 3 copies, on 2 types of media, with 1 copy kept offsite.

stem: What is the purpose of locking the screen automatically after a few minutes of inactivity?

choices.text: To prevent unauthorized access if the device is left unattended
To speed up updates
To save mobile data
To improve photo quality

explanation: Auto-lock protects an unattended device by requiring a passcode or biometrics.

stem: You receive an unexpected attachment from an unknown sender. What should you do?

choices.text: Forward it to all your contacts
Leave it unopened and treat it as suspicious, it could be malware
Open it to check what's inside
Reply asking for the password

explanation: An unexpected attachment may contain malware: it should not be opened.

stem: After using an account on a shared computer, what habit should you adopt?

choices.text: Lower the screen brightness
Just close the browser tab
Log out of the session before leaving
Leave the session open to save time

explanation: Logging out prevents the next user from reaching an account that is still open.

stem: Which of these counts as a "second factor" of authentication?

choices.text: The username
The account's email address
A more complex password
A one-time code generated by an app

explanation: A one-time code (TOTP) is a factor distinct from the password, hence the "second" factor.

stem: Unlocking with a fingerprint or facial recognition belongs to which type of factor?

choices.text: Something you have
Somewhere you are
Something you are (biometrics)
Something you know

explanation: Biometrics corresponds to the "something you are" factor.

stem: Why do updates matter not just for the operating system, but for apps as well?

choices.text: Because it changes the wallpaper automatically
Because apps that aren't updated use more Wi-Fi
Because it deletes the other apps
Because apps can also contain flaws that need fixing

explanation: Apps have vulnerabilities too, and their updates are what fix them.

stem: Why should you install software updates promptly?

choices.text: There's no point in it
To change the interface colors
To drain the battery faster
Because they often fix known security flaws

explanation: Patches close vulnerabilities that are already known and being exploited.

stem: Which password deserves particular care when you use a password manager?

choices.text: The router's password
The SIM card's PIN code
The guest network's password
The master password that protects the vault

explanation: The master password unlocks the entire vault: it must be strong and known to you alone.

stem: On a smartphone, which location permission setting best limits data collection?

choices.text: Turning off the screen lock
Allowing access only while the app is in use
Allowing it for every app at once
Allowing it always, even in the background

explanation: Restricting access to "while using the app" prevents background tracking.

stem: Why is it advisable to test restoring your backups from time to time?

choices.text: To improve screen speed
To delete old files automatically
To confirm they are actually usable when you need them
To increase the size of the hard drive

explanation: A backup is only worth something if you can genuinely restore the data from it.

stem: Which type of attachment calls for particular caution?

choices.text: An email signature containing a logo
A link to your bank's official website that you already know
A plain photo shown in the message preview
An executable file (.exe) or a document asking you to enable macros

explanation: Executables and macros are classic infection vectors.

stem: Why doesn't "closing the tab" replace a proper logout?

choices.text: Because the session can stay active and be reused
Because it turns off Wi-Fi
Because it erases the entire browser history
Because it deletes the account permanently

explanation: Closing the tab can leave the session open on the server side, unlike an explicit logout.

stem: What are the recovery codes provided when you enable 2FA for?

choices.text: Logging in if you lose access to your second factor
Speeding up your usual login
Sharing your account with a friend
Temporarily disabling the antivirus

explanation: Recovery codes let you regain access if your phone or 2FA key is lost.

stem: Which auto-lock delay setting is the most prudent on a phone?

choices.text: A delay of several hours
Never, to avoid having to type the passcode
A short delay, on the order of a minute
Only at night

explanation: A short delay shortens the window during which a forgotten device stays unlocked.

stem: What happens if you ignore update notifications for a long time?

choices.text: The battery charges faster
Known flaws stay open and exploitable
Passwords reset themselves
The device gets faster over time

explanation: Without updates, already-published vulnerabilities remain exploitable by attackers.

stem: How does a password manager's autofill also help against phishing?

choices.text: It offers the password even on a fake site
It only offers the credentials on the legitimate domain that was saved
It encrypts the Internet connection
It sends an alert to the bank

explanation: Autofill only triggers on the exact domain that was saved, so it stays silent on a fraudulent site imitating the address.

stem: An app requests a permission it clearly does not need. Which principle should you apply?

choices.text: The principle of speed: accept to save time
The principle of least privilege: grant only what is strictly necessary
The all-or-nothing principle: allow everything at once
The backup principle: make a copy before accepting

explanation: Least privilege means granting only the permissions that are essential to the function.

stem: What does two-factor authentication (2FA) ask for at sign-in?

choices.text: A longer password than usual
The name of the Internet service provider
A second verification factor in addition to the password
Only the account's email address

explanation: 2FA adds a second proof of identity (a code, an app, a key) beyond the password alone.

stem: Why keep at least one offline (disconnected) backup of your data?

choices.text: So that it updates more often
To save battery life
To share files more easily
So that it stays out of reach of ransomware that encrypts connected devices

explanation: A disconnected backup escapes encryption by ransomware that hits online storage.

stem: Among these 2FA methods, which one is generally considered the most secure?

choices.text: A code given over the phone to support
An authenticator app (TOTP) or a security key
An answer to a security question
A code sent by SMS

explanation: A TOTP app or a hardware key resists interception far better than an SMS code.

stem: Why is it important to install security patches promptly?

choices.text: They increase the device's storage capacity
They fix known flaws before they are exploited
They make the battery perform better
They automatically remove ads

explanation: A patch closes an already disclosed vulnerability, shrinking the window of attack.

stem: What is the benefit of enabling automatic system updates?

choices.text: It permanently prevents every virus
It applies patches without relying on your vigilance
It speeds up the Wi-Fi connection
It encrypts all your personal files

explanation: Automatic updates make sure patches get applied without anyone forgetting.

stem: What is a password manager mainly used for?

choices.text: Storing and generating unique, strong passwords
Sharing your credentials publicly
Replacing the computer's antivirus
Blocking unwanted calls

explanation: A manager stores unique passwords in encrypted form and can generate strong ones for you.

stem: What concrete advantage does a password manager bring?

choices.text: It remembers a different password for every service on your behalf
It removes the need to lock your phone
It means you no longer need an antivirus
It lets you safely use the same password everywhere

explanation: It stores a distinct password for each account, with nothing for you to memorize.

stem: Why avoid reusing the same password across several sites?

choices.text: Because it uses up more memory
Because every site forbids it
Because it slows down the connection
Because a breach on one site then exposes all your other accounts

explanation: A reused password, once leaked, opens the door to every account it protects (credential stuffing).

stem: The padlock and "https://" in the browser mainly indicate that…

choices.text: The site is guaranteed virus-free
The connection to the site is encrypted
The site belongs to an official company
The site is free

explanation: HTTPS encrypts the transport. It does NOT guarantee that the site is honest — a malicious site can use HTTPS too.

stem: What does the term "malware" refer to?

choices.text: A badly designed web page
Any software designed to cause harm or act without your knowledge
A computer that has become too slow
Paid software that costs too much

explanation: "Malware" is the umbrella term for any malicious software.

stem: Software that encrypts your files and then demands a ransom is called…

choices.text: A browser
A firewall
A cookie
Ransomware

explanation: Ransomware locks up your data and demands a payment to release it.

stem: Which of the following is a type of malware?

choices.text: An IP address
A browser cache
A Trojan horse (trojan)
An RSS feed

explanation: A Trojan horse is malware disguised as a legitimate program.

stem: What is a common way for malware to reach your device?

choices.text: Setting the system clock
Simply turning on the screen
A booby-trapped email attachment
Changing the wallpaper

explanation: Malicious attachments and links are a classic infection vector.

stem: A pop-up appears: "Your computer is infected, call this Microsoft number." What should you do?

choices.text: Pay for urgent repair service
Give the "technician" remote access
Ignore the message, it is a tech support scam
Call the displayed number immediately

explanation: These alarmist pop-ups are fake tech support scams trying to trap you.

stem: How can you recognize a tech support scam?

choices.text: It offers to refund you with no conditions whatsoever
It manufactures urgency and demands access or payment
It never asks for anything in return
It always arrives by postal mail

explanation: Fake support plays on fear to obtain money or access to your device.

stem: You receive: "You've won an iPhone, click here to claim it." What is this most likely?

choices.text: A security update
A fake prize scam (lottery or giveaway)
An official message from the manufacturer
A gift genuinely won in a prize draw

explanation: Fake prizes are bait used to steal data or money.

stem: Your "bank" calls and asks you to transfer your funds to a "secure account." What should you do?

choices.text: Make the transfer right away
Follow the caller's instructions
Hang up: a real bank never asks for that
Give out your verification code

explanation: Asking for a transfer to a "secure account" is a classic wire transfer fraud.

stem: An email impersonates your bank and asks for your credentials through a link. What is this called?

choices.text: Private browsing
A firewall
Quarantine
Phishing

explanation: Phishing impersonates a trusted entity in order to steal your credentials.

stem: What does your browser's private browsing mode actually do?

choices.text: It blocks all viruses
It does not save local history on this device
It makes you completely anonymous on the Internet
It prevents your accounts from ever being hacked

explanation: Private browsing mainly wipes local traces; it does not make you anonymous.

stem: What is the main role of a firewall?

choices.text: Filtering inbound and outbound network traffic according to rules
Removing spam from the mailbox
Backing up files automatically
Speeding up the Internet connection

explanation: A firewall allows or blocks connections based on rules (ports, addresses, protocols).

stem: In private browsing, who can still see the sites you visit?

choices.text: Only you, never the sites you visit
Your Internet provider and your employer or network
Only the other open tabs
Absolutely no one

explanation: Private browsing does not hide your activity from the network or your Internet provider.

stem: Does private browsing hide your IP address from the sites you visit?

choices.text: Yes, but only on weekends
No, because IP addresses do not exist
Yes, your IP address becomes invisible
No, sites still see your IP address

explanation: Private browsing does not anonymize your IP address from the sites you visit.

stem: What does it mean for an antivirus to "quarantine" a file?

choices.text: Isolating the suspicious file so it can no longer do anything
Sharing it with your contacts for a second opinion
Compressing it to free up space
Sending it to the police immediately

explanation: Quarantine isolates the suspicious file to keep it from doing harm.

stem: Is a quarantined file permanently deleted?

choices.text: Yes, it is erased instantly
No, it is isolated and can often be restored or deleted later
Yes, and the drive is reformatted right afterwards
No, it keeps running normally

explanation: Quarantine sets the file aside without executing it; you can then restore or erase it.

stem: Before entering your password, which habit helps you avoid phishing?

choices.text: Counting the number of images on the page
Carefully checking the site's address (URL)
Watching how fast the page loads
Checking the color of the sign-in button

explanation: A spoofed URL is a common sign of phishing, so it is worth checking.

stem: Does an antivirus guarantee 100% protection?

choices.text: Yes, as long as the device is new
No, an antivirus is completely useless
Yes, no virus can get through
No, it reduces the risk but never removes it entirely

explanation: No antivirus is foolproof; vigilance is still required.

stem: Your browser shows "Not secure" before the address of a payment site. What should you do?

choices.text: Continue, the message does not matter
Turn off the browser warning
Enter your details faster to limit the risk
Do not enter any banking details on this site

explanation: The lack of a secure connection on a payment page is a red flag.

stem: Can a smartphone be infected with malware too?

choices.text: No, smartphones are immune
Yes, but only while it is switched off
Yes, notably through malicious apps or links
No, only desktop computers are

explanation: Smartphones are malware targets as well, often through malicious apps or links.

stem: Which sign should raise your suspicions in an urgent payment request received by email?

choices.text: The use of correct grammar
Being sent during business hours
The presence of a polite signature
Pressure to pay quickly and keep it confidential

explanation: Urgency and secrecy are typical markers of wire transfer fraud.

stem: What does an antivirus do during a scan of your device?

choices.text: It cuts off the Internet connection
It deletes all your photos
It changes your passwords automatically
It goes through the files looking for known threats

explanation: A scan consists of examining files to spot malware in them.

stem: A site displays "https://" and a padlock. What does that guarantee above all?

choices.text: That the site has been vetted by the police
That the site is honest and trustworthy
That your data will never be resold
That the connection between your browser and the site is encrypted

explanation: The padlock only indicates that the exchanges are encrypted, not that the site is trustworthy.

stem: In private browsing, the files you download…

choices.text: Disappear as soon as the window is closed
Are encrypted automatically
Cannot contain viruses
Stay saved on your device

explanation: Private browsing does not erase downloaded files, which remain on the device.

stem: Can a phishing site display the "https" padlock too?

choices.text: Yes, getting an https certificate is easy, even for a scammer
No, only official sites can have a padlock
No, the padlock is issued by banks
Yes, but only for a few minutes

explanation: These days https is free and quick to obtain, including for a malicious site.

stem: What information can a site using "http://" (without the "s") potentially leave exposed?

choices.text: Only the date of your visit
The data exchanged, which travels in cleartext over the network
Only the address of the site visited
Nothing, http and https protect data the same way

explanation: Without the "s", information travels in cleartext and can be intercepted.

stem: A firewall is used above all to…

choices.text: Filter which network connections are allowed in or out
Back up your important files
Speed up your Internet connection
Remove viruses already present on the drive

explanation: A firewall controls network traffic; it is not there to clean up or back up anything.

stem: Does a firewall replace an antivirus?

choices.text: Yes, a firewall does everything an antivirus does
No, firewalls are completely useless nowadays
Yes, if the firewall is a paid one
No, they have different, complementary roles

explanation: The firewall filters the network while the antivirus detects malicious programs.

stem: What is the main role of an antivirus?

choices.text: Creating passwords for your accounts
Preventing power outages
Detecting and blocking malicious software (malware)
Encrypting all your Internet communications

explanation: An antivirus spots and neutralizes malicious software.

stem: Should you keep your antivirus up to date, and why?

choices.text: Yes, so it can recognize recent threats
Yes, but only to change its color
No, updates just slow it down for nothing
No, an antivirus never needs updating

explanation: New malware appears every day, so the antivirus must be updated to know about it.

stem: What does DNS mainly do?

choices.text: It blocks network ports
It encrypts emails
It stores passwords
It translates a domain name (e.g. example.com) into an IP address

explanation: DNS is the "directory" that maps a human-readable name to the server's IP address.

stem: What is the main difference between HTTP and HTTPS?

choices.text: HTTPS encrypts the exchanges, unlike HTTP
HTTP works only on mobile
HTTPS is only used to display images
HTTPS is older than HTTP

explanation: HTTPS adds a layer of encryption (TLS) that HTTP does not have.

stem: Which port is used by default for DNS queries?
explanation: The DNS service listens on port 53 by default.

stem: What is a network protocol?

choices.text: The commercial name of an Internet service provider
A type of virus that spreads across the network
A set of shared rules that let machines communicate
A specific cable connecting two routers

explanation: A protocol defines the rules devices follow in order to exchange data correctly.

stem: Which port is generally associated with the RDP (remote desktop) protocol?
explanation: The RDP protocol uses port 3389 by default.

stem: What is the SMTP protocol mainly used for?

choices.text: Assigning IP addresses to devices
Sending email between mail servers
Displaying web pages
Securing Wi-Fi connections

explanation: SMTP is the protocol dedicated to sending and relaying email.

stem: What characterizes a public IP address?

choices.text: It exists only inside a local network
It is reachable directly from the Internet
It is the same for every device in the world
It necessarily changes every time the computer restarts

explanation: A public IP address is unique and reachable from anywhere on the Internet.

stem: Which port is used by default by the HTTP protocol (unsecured)?
explanation: HTTP traditionally uses port 80.

stem: What makes exchanges over HTTPS secure?

choices.text: The encryption of data via TLS
The compression of the site's images
The deletion of cookies
Faster page rendering

explanation: HTTPS relies on TLS encryption to protect data in transit.

stem: What is the typical format of an IPv4 address?

choices.text: A string of 32 random letters
Four numbers from 0 to 255 separated by dots
A domain name ending in .com
A sequence of six pairs of hexadecimal characters

explanation: An IPv4 address is made up of four octets, written in decimal and separated by dots.

stem: What does a DNS server typically return when you submit a domain name to it?

choices.text: The matching IP address
The website's password
The domain's creation date
The page's source code

explanation: DNS translates a domain name into its IP address so you can reach the intended server.

stem: Which port does HTTPS normally use?
explanation: 443 = HTTPS. For reference: 21 = FTP, 25 = SMTP, 3389 = RDP.

stem: Why can't a private IP address be reached directly from the internet?

choices.text: Because it is always encrypted
Because it is only valid inside the local network
Because it changes every second
Because it belongs to the internet service provider

explanation: Private addresses can only be used internally and are not routed on the internet.

stem: In network communication, what is the client's role?

choices.text: It stores every web page on the internet
It sends a request asking the server for a service
It assigns the network's IP addresses
It waits passively and never initiates a connection

explanation: The client issues a request, and the server answers by providing the requested service.

stem: What is the key difference between a MAC address and an IP address?

choices.text: The MAC address is for sending email, the IP address is for browsing
The MAC address is public, the IP address is always private
The MAC address identifies the network hardware, the IP address identifies the device on the network
The MAC address changes with every connection, the IP address is burned into the hardware

explanation: The MAC address is tied to the network card's hardware, while the IP address locates the device on the network.

stem: What is the SSH protocol mainly used for?

choices.text: Connecting to a machine remotely in a secure way
Converting domain names into IP addresses
Sending email in bulk
Displaying web pages in a browser

explanation: SSH provides encrypted remote access to a machine, most often through a command line.

stem: What does the address 127.0.0.1 refer to on a computer?

choices.text: The local machine itself (localhost)
The address of a public DNS server
The connection's public address
The address of the network's router

explanation: The address 127.0.0.1 is the loopback address, which refers to the local machine itself.

stem: Which of the following is an example of a network protocol?

choices.text: An Ethernet cable
TCP/IP
A graphics card
A hard drive

explanation: TCP/IP is a suite of protocols that governs communication over the internet.

stem: Which service automatically assigns an IP address to a device joining a network?

choices.text: HTTPS
SSH
SMTP
DHCP

explanation: The DHCP protocol automatically hands out IP addresses to devices on the network.

stem: What does the padlock shown next to a website's address usually mean?

choices.text: The site contains no advertising
The connection to the site is encrypted with HTTPS
The site is blocked by the browser
The site is hosted in France

explanation: The padlock indicates that the connection uses HTTPS and that the traffic is encrypted.

stem: Which port does the HTTPS protocol use by default?
explanation: HTTPS listens on port 443 by default.

stem: When you type an address such as example.com into a browser, what happens first?

choices.text: A DNS resolution translates the name into an IP address
The server's MAC address is encrypted
The site is downloaded immediately with no further step
The router deletes the domain name

explanation: Before contacting the server, the browser queries DNS to obtain the domain's IP address.

stem: What is an IP address mainly used for on a network?

choices.text: Encrypting the contents of communications
Storing the user's passwords
Identifying and locating a device so data can be sent to it
Measuring connection speed

explanation: The IP address identifies a device on the network so that data can be routed to it.

stem: Why do we use domain names rather than IP addresses to visit websites?

choices.text: Because IP addresses are banned on the internet
Because names are easier for humans to remember
Because domain names make the connection faster
Because IP addresses only work on a local network

explanation: Domain names are more readable and easier to remember than the strings of digits in IP addresses.

stem: Which port is normally associated with the SSH protocol?
explanation: SSH uses port 22 by default for secure remote access.

stem: Which of these addresses is a private IP address?
explanation: The 192.168.x.x range is reserved for private local networks and is not routable on the internet.

stem: What is the main role of a home router?

choices.text: Hosting the websites you visit
Forwarding data between the local network and the internet
Automatically backing up documents
Scanning files for viruses

explanation: The router connects the local network to the internet and directs packets toward their destination.

stem: Which statement best describes the difference between a LAN and the internet?

choices.text: The internet is a local network, while a LAN is worldwide
A LAN is a limited local network, while the internet connects networks on a global scale
A LAN is by definition slower than the internet
A LAN only works over Wi-Fi, and the internet only over cable

explanation: A LAN covers a limited local area, whereas the internet interconnects networks all over the world.

stem: What is a MAC address?

choices.text: The Wi-Fi access password
A website's domain name
The operating system's serial number
A unique physical identifier assigned to a network interface card

explanation: The MAC address is a unique hardware identifier burned into the network interface.

stem: What is a server on a network?

choices.text: An IP address reserved for the printer
A machine that provides resources or services to other machines
A cable connecting two computers
Antivirus software installed on the workstation

explanation: A server makes available the services or resources requested by clients.

stem: What is ransomware?

choices.text: A password manager
A free antivirus
Software that encrypts your files and demands a ransom to give them back
A hardware firewall

explanation: Ransomware makes data inaccessible (by encrypting it) and demands a payment.

stem: What is the goal of a social engineering attack?

choices.text: Exploiting a technical flaw in a web server's code
Cracking an encryption key by brute force
Psychologically manipulating a person into revealing information or performing an action
Flooding a site with requests until it becomes unavailable

explanation: Social engineering targets people rather than technology, manipulating the victim into handing over information or taking a risky action.

stem: What characterizes a rootkit?

choices.text: It displays a ransom demand on the victim's screen
It hides its own presence and that of other malware to stay undetected on the system
It boosts system performance by deleting temporary files
It does nothing more than send bulk phishing emails

explanation: A rootkit's primary aim is to conceal its presence and other malicious activity in order to evade detection.

stem: Does a VPN protect you against a fraudulent (phishing) website that you willingly hand your password to?

choices.text: Yes, because a VPN verifies the true identity of every site you visit
Yes, a VPN automatically detects and blocks all phishing sites
No, a VPN encrypts traffic but does not stop you from entering your credentials on a fake site
No, because a VPN completely disables the browser on unknown sites

explanation: A VPN secures and encrypts the connection but makes no judgement about a site's legitimacy: it cannot stop you from willingly handing your data to a fraudulent site.

stem: What is adware?

choices.text: A legitimate tool for managing marketing campaigns
Software that displays unwanted advertising, often to generate revenue
Software that blocks all advertising on web pages
An antivirus specialized in detecting ransomware

explanation: Adware displays unsolicited advertising, usually to pay its authors, and can degrade both the user experience and privacy.

stem: Why keep at least one backup copy offsite (outside the premises)?

choices.text: To reduce backup size through compression
To speed up file restoration
To protect the data from a local disaster such as a fire, theft or flood
Because the law forbids storing two copies in the same place

explanation: An offsite copy survives an incident that would destroy every copy held on the premises, such as a fire or a theft.

stem: Faced with ransomware that has encrypted your files, which measure lets you recover the data without paying?

choices.text: Restoring the files from a clean, disconnected backup
Rebooting the machine several times to undo the encryption
Renaming the encrypted files to restore their original extension
Disabling the antivirus in order to unlock access to the files

explanation: Having a recent, clean and isolated backup lets you restore the data without giving in to the ransom demand.

stem: An email claims to be from your bank and asks you to "confirm" your password through a link. What kind of attack is this?

choices.text: A denial-of-service attack
An SQL injection
Phishing, a common form of social engineering
A buffer overflow

explanation: Phishing impersonates a trusted party to push the victim into disclosing their credentials, which makes it a form of social engineering.

stem: Why can a single piece of malware combine several types of behavior (a Trojan that installs a keylogger, for example)?

choices.text: Because the antivirus randomly renames the threats it finds
Because a file can only belong to one category at a time
Because the operating system automatically merges the malware it detects
Because the categories describe functions, and one piece of malware can combine several of them

explanation: Malware families describe behaviors (propagation, concealment, theft) that a single program can combine, such as a Trojan that drops a keylogger.

stem: What does a computer that has been enlisted into a botnet typically do without its owner's knowledge?

choices.text: It uninstalls the other malware present on the machine
It automatically encrypts all of its data to protect itself
It carries out orders received from a command server, for example taking part in a DDoS attack
It sends security reports to the antivirus vendor

explanation: A botnet "zombie" machine obeys the command-and-control (C2) server, for example to send spam or take part in a distributed attack.

stem: What is the benefit of backing up to two different types of media (for example an external drive and the cloud)?

choices.text: Forcing the user to enter two separate passwords
Automatically doubling the data write speed
Making it possible to permanently delete older versions
Reducing the risk of losing everything if one type of media fails or is compromised

explanation: Using two types of media prevents a single failure (hardware fault, disaster, attack) from destroying every copy at once.

stem: What is a VPN mainly used for?

choices.text: Adding more RAM
Defragmenting the disk
Creating an encrypted tunnel between your device and a remote network
Deleting cookies

explanation: A VPN encrypts traffic inside a tunnel that runs to a remote exit point.

stem: Which statement best describes the propagation component of a worm such as Conficker or WannaCry?

choices.text: It exploits network vulnerabilities to copy itself automatically to other machines
It waits for an administrator to install it willingly
It can only spread by being copied manually onto a USB drive
It only works on an isolated machine with no network

explanation: A worm replicates on its own by exploiting network vulnerabilities to reach other systems without any human intervention.

stem: On an unencrypted public Wi-Fi network, what does using a VPN actually protect?

choices.text: It permanently prevents any malware from being installed on the device
It makes the computer invisible to system updates
It deletes weak passwords stored in the browser
It encrypts your traffic so that someone else on the same network cannot intercept it in clear text

explanation: A VPN encrypts the traffic between the device and the VPN server, which prevents an attacker on the same network from reading the exchanges.

stem: What is pretexting, a social engineering technique?

choices.text: Automatically scanning a machine's open ports
Encrypting a message so that only the recipient can read it
Inventing a believable scenario (a fake identity, a fake reason) to gain the victim's trust
Injecting malicious code into a web page

explanation: Pretexting means building a convincing false scenario, often with a stolen identity, to manipulate the victim into giving up information.

stem: Why is a backup that stays permanently connected to the machine risky when it comes to ransomware?

choices.text: A permanent connection prevents any data compression
A permanently connected backup can never be restored
The ransomware can encrypt the backup too, since it remains accessible
Ransomware only attacks disconnected media

explanation: Ransomware encrypts everything it can reach: a backup that stays plugged in can be encrypted along with the original data.

stem: Which situation best illustrates a baiting attack?

choices.text: A security patch installed automatically by the system
A password guessed through automated repeated attempts
A server overwhelmed by millions of simultaneous requests
A USB drive left in a parking lot that the victim plugs in out of curiosity

explanation: Baiting exploits curiosity by leaving an attractive "bait", such as a booby-trapped USB drive, that the victim will use of their own accord.

stem: Once a rootkit is installed at the system level, what problem does it pose for defenders?

choices.text: It displays a visible alert announcing its own presence
It only affects external peripherals, never the system
It uninstalls itself automatically after a few minutes
It hides its files and processes, making detection with the usual tools difficult

explanation: A rootkit embeds itself deep in the system and conceals its files and processes, which makes it very hard to detect and remove.

stem: Does a VPN guarantee complete anonymity online?

choices.text: Yes, a VPN makes all activity completely untraceable under any circumstances
No, because a VPN never changes the IP address that others see
Yes, because a VPN automatically erases all browsing history
No, it hides your IP address but the VPN provider and your logins to accounts can still identify you

explanation: A VPN hides your real IP address, but the VPN operator can see the traffic, and logging into personal accounts still allows identification.

stem: What is the main function of a command-and-control (C2) server in a botnet?

choices.text: Blocking suspicious outbound connections
Automatically backing up the victims' files
Legitimately distributing operating system updates
Relaying the attacker's instructions to the infected machines and receiving their data

explanation: The C2 (command and control) server acts as the control center: it sends orders to the bots and collects the information they send back.

stem: A colleague gets a call from someone claiming to be "IT support" and asking for their password. What is the appropriate response?

choices.text: Transfer the call to another colleague without asking questions
Give out the password, since support needs it
Refuse to give the password and verify the caller's identity through an official channel
Change the password immediately and read the new one out to the caller

explanation: Legitimate support never asks for a password; you should refuse and verify the caller's identity through an official channel.

stem: Why can a payload stay "dormant" for some time after the infection?

choices.text: It needs the user to approve it manually every day
It must first be compiled by the victim's antivirus
It only activates when the computer is switched off
It waits for a triggering condition, such as a date or a command, before executing

explanation: Some payloads stay inactive until a defined trigger (a logic bomb, a date, an order from the C2) in order to delay detection.

stem: What is the main difference between a virus and a worm?

choices.text: There is no technical difference, the two terms are strictly synonymous
The virus spreads on its own across the network, while the worm needs a host file
The virus can only infect servers, and the worm only workstations
The worm spreads on its own across the network, while the virus needs a host file and a user action

explanation: A worm replicates and spreads autonomously across the network, whereas a virus must attach itself to a host file and be run by the user.

stem: Which of these types of malware has the primary goal of extorting money by blocking access to data?

choices.text: Adware
Spyware
Ransomware
Rootkit

explanation: Ransomware encrypts or blocks the data and then demands a ransom in exchange for giving it back, unlike the other families listed.

stem: What is a keylogger?

choices.text: A system log that records incoming network connections
A program that records keystrokes in order to steal credentials and passwords
A tool that encrypts the contents of the hard drive to protect it
Software that speeds up typing through autocompletion

explanation: A keylogger quietly captures the keys typed in order to harvest sensitive information such as passwords.

stem: What does the 3-2-1 backup rule refer to?

choices.text: Encrypting data with 3 algorithms, 2 keys and 1 certificate
Keeping 3 passwords, 2 USB drives and 1 antivirus
Keeping 3 copies of the data, on 2 different types of media, with 1 copy offsite
Running 3 backups a day, for 2 weeks, on 1 single disk

explanation: The 3-2-1 rule recommends 3 copies of the data, on 2 different types of media, with at least 1 copy stored offsite.

stem: What is a botnet?

choices.text: A set of official servers belonging to an email provider
Backup software that replicates data across several sites
An automated firewall that blocks web crawlers
A network of infected machines controlled remotely by an attacker

explanation: A botnet is a collection of compromised computers (bots) driven remotely by an attacker, often to launch attacks or send spam.

stem: In the context of malware, what does the payload refer to?

choices.text: The amount of bandwidth consumed by the malware's propagation
The digital certificate signing the executable file
The part of the code that carries out the actual malicious action once the system is compromised
The list of IP addresses the malware refuses to attack

explanation: The payload is the portion of the malware that delivers the intended harmful effect, such as encrypting files or stealing data.

stem: What is the main purpose of a Trojan horse?

choices.text: Displaying intrusive ads in the browser
Replicating automatically from machine to machine without intervention
Blocking the operating system's security updates
Passing itself off as legitimate software to trick the user into installing it

explanation: A Trojan disguises itself as a legitimate program to fool the user into installing it, then opens the door to malicious actions.

stem: What is spyware?

choices.text: Software that quietly collects information about the user without their knowledge
A program that automatically deletes tracking cookies
Software that encrypts communications to protect privacy
A network monitoring tool used only by administrators

explanation: Spyware spies on the user and sends their data (habits, credentials) to a third party without their consent.

stem: Which statement describes symmetric encryption?

choices.text: Only the recipient holds a key
The same key is used to encrypt and to decrypt
It uses two distinct keys, one public and one private
It requires no key at all

explanation: Symmetric = a single shared key (e.g. AES). Asymmetric encryption uses a public/private key pair.

stem: To send a confidential message to Alice using an asymmetric system, which key does the sender use?

choices.text: Alice's private key
A secret key shared in advance
Their own private key
Alice's public key

explanation: You encrypt with the recipient's public key, and only they can decrypt it with their private key.

stem: Which key does a person use to apply a digital signature to a document?

choices.text: The recipient's public key
A symmetric session key
Their private key
Their public key

explanation: A signature is generated with the signer's private key and verified with their public key.

stem: What does collision resistance of a hash function mean?

choices.text: The digest can be reversed if you know the algorithm
The hashing key cannot be guessed
It is extremely hard to find two different inputs that produce the same digest
Two identical files produce different digests

explanation: Collision resistance makes it computationally infeasible to find two distinct inputs with the same digest.

stem: What is the main drawback of symmetric encryption?

choices.text: It cannot encrypt large volumes of data
It is slow compared to asymmetric encryption
It uses no key whatsoever
The secret key must be distributed securely

explanation: The main challenge with symmetric encryption is exchanging the secret key securely between the parties.

stem: Why are asymmetric and symmetric encryption often combined in secure communications?

choices.text: To do away with keys entirely
Symmetric encryption exchanges the key and asymmetric encryption encrypts the data
Asymmetric encryption exchanges the key and symmetric encryption encrypts the data quickly
To systematically double the key size

explanation: Asymmetric encryption is used to exchange a session key, then symmetric encryption efficiently encrypts the data.

stem: What does a digital signature primarily guarantee?

choices.text: The confidentiality of the message content
The authenticity and integrity of the message
Complete anonymity of the sender
Compression of the message

explanation: A digital signature attests to the sender's authenticity and to the integrity of the message.

stem: Why is a password's hash stored rather than the password in cleartext?

choices.text: To make it easier to transmit in Base64
Because hashing compresses the password
So it can be decrypted more quickly later
So the password is not revealed if the database is breached

explanation: Storing the hash avoids exposing the cleartext password if the database is leaked.

stem: What happens if you hash exactly the same data twice with SHA-256?

choices.text: You get two different digests
You get exactly the same digest
You get a digest twice as long
The digest computation fails

explanation: A hash function is deterministic: the same input always produces the same digest.

stem: What happens when a single bit of the input to a good hash function is changed?

choices.text: A radically different digest
A shorter digest
A digest almost identical to the previous one
No change to the digest

explanation: Thanks to the avalanche effect, a tiny change to the input completely transforms the digest.

stem: How do you recover the original data from a Base64-encoded string?

choices.text: It is impossible, the operation is one-way
By decoding it, with no key at all
By decrypting it with the public key
By decrypting it with the private key

explanation: Base64 is reversible: simply decoding it, without any key, restores the original data.

stem: Which property characterizes a cryptographic hash function?

choices.text: It encrypts data so it can be decrypted later
The input is easily recovered from the digest
It is extremely hard to find two inputs with the same digest
It is reversible with the right key

explanation: Collision resistance + one-way: a hash is not encryption (it cannot be reversed).

stem: Which of these algorithms is not a hash function?

choices.text: SHA-1
AES
SHA-256
MD5

explanation: AES is an encryption algorithm, whereas SHA-1, SHA-256 and MD5 are hash functions.

stem: Which statement about a public key is true?

choices.text: It must be kept secret, just like the private key
It is strictly identical to the private key
It is used to sign documents instead of the private key
It can be distributed freely

explanation: The public key is meant to be shared widely, unlike the private key, which stays secret.

stem: Which key is used to verify a sender's digital signature?

choices.text: The verifier's private key
The sender's private key
A shared symmetric key
The sender's public key

explanation: A signature is verified with the sender's public key, which matches the private key that signed it.

stem: Why is MD5 discouraged for modern security purposes?

choices.text: It produces a far too long digest
Collisions can be produced in practice
It requires a public key to work
It encrypts data instead of hashing it

explanation: MD5 is broken: practical collision generation makes it unsuitable for security purposes.

stem: What characterizes a block cipher such as AES?

choices.text: It works without any key
It encrypts data bit by bit as a continuous stream
It processes data in fixed-size groups of bits
It produces a fixed-size digest

explanation: A block cipher such as AES processes data in fixed-size blocks.

stem: What is the primary purpose of encryption?

choices.text: To guarantee the confidentiality of data
To guarantee integrity by means of a digest
To make data readable by anyone
To reduce the size of transmitted data

explanation: Encryption is first and foremost about preserving the confidentiality of data.

stem: What is the purpose of a salt added before hashing a password?

choices.text: To shorten the resulting digest
To encode the password in Base64
To encrypt the password with a key
To make the digests of identical passwords unique

explanation: A salt ensures that two identical passwords produce different digests, defeating rainbow tables.

stem: Which of these operations ensures the confidentiality of a file?

choices.text: Hashing it with SHA-256
Encoding it in Base64
Encrypting it with AES
Computing its CRC checksum

explanation: Only encryption, here AES, protects confidentiality; hashing, Base64 and CRC do not.

stem: How many keys make up an asymmetric key pair?

choices.text: As many keys as there are recipients
Three completely independent keys
Two mathematically related keys, one public and one private
A single key shared between the parties

explanation: An asymmetric system relies on a pair: a public key and a private key that are mathematically related.

stem: Why is hashing unsuitable for protecting a message that the recipient must be able to read?

choices.text: Because the message cannot be reconstructed from the digest
Because hashing is far too slow
Because the resulting digest is too long
Because hashing requires a public key

explanation: Since hashing is irreversible, the recipient could never recover the original message.

stem: In symmetric encryption, which key is used both to encrypt and to decrypt?

choices.text: A different random key for each byte, never shared
The same secret key shared between the parties
No key is needed
A public key to encrypt and a private key to decrypt

explanation: Symmetric encryption uses a single shared secret key to both encrypt and decrypt.

stem: If a digitally signed document is modified after signing, what happens during verification?

choices.text: The signature remains perfectly valid
Signature verification fails
The document is decrypted automatically
The document's digest stays the same

explanation: Any later modification invalidates the signature, which reveals that the document has been tampered with.

stem: Which category does the AES algorithm belong to?

choices.text: Symmetric block cipher
Asymmetric public-key encryption
Cryptographic hash function
Data compression protocol

explanation: AES is a widely used symmetric block cipher.

stem: What is the RSA algorithm mainly used for?

choices.text: Symmetric stream encryption
Lossless data compression
Fast hashing of large files
Asymmetric encryption and signing with a key pair

explanation: RSA is an asymmetric algorithm used for encryption and signing via a key pair.

stem: What is the size of the digest produced by SHA-256?

choices.text: A 128-bit digest
A 256-byte digest
A variable size depending on the input
A 256-bit digest

explanation: SHA-256 always produces a 256-bit digest, regardless of the input size.

stem: Why can't the original message be recovered from its hash digest?

choices.text: Because the digest is encrypted with a key
Because the digest is encoded in Base64
Because a hash function is designed to be one-way
Because you need to hold the private key

explanation: A hash function is one-way: the digest cannot be used to reconstruct the input.

stem: What does Base64 encoding of data guarantee?

choices.text: Integrity verifiable through a digest
Confidentiality thanks to a secret key
Authentication of the sender
An ASCII character representation, with no confidentiality whatsoever

explanation: Base64 is a simple keyless, reversible encoding: it provides no confidentiality.

stem: What is the fundamental difference between encryption and hashing?

choices.text: Encryption produces a fixed-size output, hashing does not
Hashing requires a public key, encryption does not
Encryption is reversible with the key, hashing is not
Encryption is always faster than hashing

explanation: Encryption is reversible for whoever holds the key, whereas hashing is irreversible.

stem: Why is a salt added before hashing a password?

choices.text: So that two identical passwords produce different digests and to defeat rainbow tables
To encrypt the password reversibly
To speed up hashing
To shorten the digest

explanation: A per-user unique salt breaks precomputed tables (rainbow tables).

stem: What distinguishes a dictionary attack from a brute-force attack?

choices.text: It only works on unsalted hashes
It always requires physical access to the machine
It targets the network rather than the password
It tries a list of likely words rather than every possible combination

explanation: A dictionary attack tries likely passwords (common words, leaked ones) instead of sweeping the entire character space.

stem: Why is a dictionary attack often faster than pure brute force?

choices.text: Because it exploits the fact that many people choose predictable passwords
Because it disables rate limiting on login attempts
Because it always uses several machines in parallel
Because it bypasses the server's hashing

explanation: By testing predictable human passwords first, it often finds the answer without covering the entire search space.

stem: What is a session cookie used for on a website?

choices.text: Blocking requests coming from other domains
Keeping the user authenticated across successive requests
Storing the password in cleartext on the server
Encrypting all HTTPS traffic

explanation: The session cookie holds an identifier that lets the server recognize the already-authenticated user on every request.

stem: Session hijacking allows an attacker to…

choices.text: Impersonate the victim without knowing their password
Modify the hash stored on the server
Disable the victim's firewall
Decrypt the victim's TLS communications

explanation: By stealing a valid session identifier, the attacker is recognized as the victim without ever supplying their credentials.

stem: What is the HttpOnly attribute on a session cookie for?

choices.text: Preventing the cookie from being read by client-side JavaScript
Encrypting the cookie's contents
Limiting the cookie's lifetime to one hour
Forcing the cookie to be sent over HTTPS only

explanation: HttpOnly makes the cookie inaccessible to JavaScript, which reduces the risk of theft through an XSS flaw.

stem: A cookie's Secure attribute guarantees that it…

choices.text: Always expires when the browser is closed
Is cryptographically signed by the server
Cannot be deleted by the user
Is only sent over an encrypted HTTPS connection

explanation: The Secure flag prevents the cookie from being sent in cleartext over HTTP, restricting it to encrypted connections.

stem: In the context of web authentication, what is a token?

choices.text: The encrypted username
A hardware component required in order to log in
The hashed password stored in the database
A value issued after login that proves identity on subsequent requests

explanation: A token is proof of authentication issued after login, then presented to access resources without resending the password.

stem: If an access token is intercepted by an attacker, the main risk is that…

choices.text: The victim's password is changed automatically
The database is encrypted by ransomware
The server is taken offline
The attacker can act on the victim's behalf for as long as the token is valid

explanation: An intercepted token lets an attacker impersonate the victim until it expires or is revoked.

stem: Why is the validity period of an access token often kept short?

choices.text: To prevent malicious SQL queries
To save disk space on the server
To reduce the window during which a stolen token remains usable
To speed up data encryption

explanation: A short lifetime limits how long an attacker can reuse a stolen token.

stem: What sets spear phishing apart from ordinary phishing?

choices.text: It only works over SMS
It exploits software flaws only, with no human interaction
It targets a specific person with a tailored message
It sends millions of identical emails at random

explanation: Spear phishing targets a specific victim with a message tailored to their context, which makes it far more convincing.

stem: SQL injection mainly exploits…

choices.text: A missing browser update
A faulty network cable
Poorly sanitized user input that gets interpreted as SQL code
A password that is too weak

explanation: Without parameterized queries, user input can alter the SQL query the database executes.

stem: Before a spear phishing attack, the attacker often gathers information about the target through…

choices.text: An SQL injection in their browser
Physically stealing their computer
A brute-force attack on their passwords
Social media and publicly available information (OSINT)

explanation: The attacker relies on public sources (OSINT) to personalize the message and gain the target's trust.

stem: Spear phishing aimed specifically at a company executive is commonly called…

choices.text: Smishing
Pharming
Vishing
Whaling

explanation: Whaling is spear phishing aimed at senior executives, whose compromise has a major impact.

stem: In security, enumeration consists of…

choices.text: Actively collecting detailed information about a system (accounts, services, shares)
Encrypting the files on a targeted system
Deleting a server's logs
Generating random passwords

explanation: Enumeration actively extracts details from a system (usernames, services, resources) to prepare an attack.

stem: A login message stating "this username does not exist" makes it easier to…

choices.text: Detect SQL injections
Rotate tokens automatically
Enumerate valid accounts
Encrypt sessions

explanation: Error messages that differ depending on whether the account exists let an attacker determine which usernames are valid.

stem: To prevent account enumeration, a login form should…

choices.text: Display the same error message whether or not the username exists
Display the hash of the password that was entered
Indicate whether the password is almost correct
Reveal the number of attempts remaining

explanation: A generic, identical error message prevents the attacker from telling existing accounts from non-existent ones.

stem: Why are default passwords on devices (routers, cameras) dangerous?

choices.text: They are publicly documented and often never changed
They change automatically every day
They are too long to memorize
They cannot be hashed

explanation: Default credentials are listed in public manuals and, if left unchanged, grant immediate access.

stem: Many connected devices (IoT) have been enlisted into botnets mainly because…

choices.text: They used tokens that were too long
Their default admin credentials had never been changed
Their traffic was not encrypted with TLS
They were protected by two-factor authentication

explanation: Botnets such as Mirai exploited the never-changed default credentials of thousands of connected devices.

stem: Credential stuffing consists of…

choices.text: Trying leaked username/password pairs on many different sites
Trying every possible combination of characters
Recovering a password using a rainbow table
Injecting SQL into a login form

explanation: Credential stuffing replays credentials from breaches, betting on password reuse.

stem: Why is reusing the same password across multiple sites risky?

choices.text: It disables session cookies
A breach of a single site exposes every account sharing that password
It slows down page loading
It prevents server-side hashing

explanation: If one site is compromised, the stolen credentials can be used to access every other account that uses the same password.

stem: Which measure most directly limits the effectiveness of an online brute-force attack?

choices.text: Reducing the salt length
Limiting the number of login attempts (rate limiting or account lockout)
Storing passwords in Base64
Extending the lifetime of cookies

explanation: Restricting the number of attempts per account or per IP makes online brute forcing far too slow to succeed.

stem: What is the main purpose of a salt when storing hashed passwords?

choices.text: To make the password take longer to type
To compress the hash and save disk space
To prevent two users with the same password from having the same hash
To encrypt the password so it can be decrypted later

explanation: A salt, unique per user, guarantees different hashes even for identical passwords and defeats rainbow tables.

stem: Why are deliberately slow hash functions such as bcrypt used for passwords?

choices.text: To deliberately slow down offline cracking attempts
To speed up verification on the server side
To reduce the size of the stored hash
To encrypt the password in a reversible way

explanation: A deliberately expensive hash increases the time required for each attempt, making large-scale offline cracking impractical.

stem: Where is a password salt usually kept?

choices.text: In plaintext, next to the hash in the database
Only in the user's cookie
It is never stored; it is recomputed at every login
Encrypted with the server's private key

explanation: The salt is not a secret: it is stored in plaintext alongside the hash because its role is uniqueness, not confidentiality.

stem: A rainbow table mainly allows an attacker to…

choices.text: Bypass two-factor authentication
Inject code into an SQL query
Intercept encrypted network traffic
Recover a password from its hash using a precomputed table

explanation: A rainbow table maps precomputed hashes to their passwords in order to quickly reverse an unsalted hash.

stem: SQL injection relies on the fact that…

choices.text: The administrator password is too short
The database is poorly encrypted at rest
The server runs too many queries in parallel
Data supplied by the user is interpreted as SQL code

explanation: SQL injection occurs when unfiltered user input is concatenated into the query and interpreted as SQL statements.

stem: What is the most effective protection against SQL injection?

choices.text: Using parameterized queries (prepared statements)
Limiting the number of connections per second
Encrypting passwords with a salt
Renaming the users table

explanation: Parameterized queries separate SQL code from data, preventing user input from being interpreted as statements.

stem: In a login form, entering ' OR '1'='1 into a field aims to…

choices.text: Create an always-true condition in order to bypass authentication
Steal the administrator's session cookie
Encrypt the victim's database
Crash the web server by overloading it

explanation: This payload turns the WHERE clause into an always-true condition, which can grant login without a valid password.

stem: What does a brute-force attack on a password consist of?

choices.text: Tricking the victim into revealing their password
Systematically trying every possible combination of characters
Reusing leaked credentials on another site
Testing a list of common words taken from a file

explanation: Brute force exhaustively walks through the space of possible combinations until it finds the right password.

stem: A Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) attack mainly consists of…

choices.text: Overloading a server with requests
Breaking an encryption algorithm
Injecting malicious JavaScript that runs in other users' browsers
Guessing a password by brute force

explanation: XSS targets the client: the script runs in the victim's browser (session theft, etc.).

stem: The HTTP Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS) header makes it possible to…

choices.text: Prevent the site from being displayed inside an iframe.
Forbid the execution of the inline scripts present on the page.
Force the browser to access the site over HTTPS only, for a defined period.
Limit the cookies sent on cross-site requests.

explanation: HSTS forces the browser to use HTTPS for every connection to the domain during the declared max-age, preventing downgrades to HTTP.

stem: Privilege separation in the design of a service consists of…

choices.text: Requiring two different passwords to open a single session.
Forbidding any user from holding more than one role.
Encrypting each of the service's configuration files separately.
Splitting the program into distinct components, each holding only the rights strictly required for its task.

explanation: Privilege separation isolates sensitive functions in low-privilege components, so that compromising one part does not grant full privileges.

stem: A DOM-based XSS is distinguished by the fact that…

choices.text: The payload is handled entirely on the client side by JavaScript manipulating the DOM, without necessarily passing through the server.
It can only target old browsers that have no JavaScript engine.
It absolutely requires a flaw in the web server in order to trigger.
The payload is always stored in a database before being executed.

explanation: DOM-based XSS arises from client-side JavaScript that writes attacker-controlled data into the DOM, without the server ever having to reflect the payload.

stem: The SameSite=Strict cookie attribute helps counter CSRF attacks because it…

choices.text: Prevents the cookie from being sent on requests initiated from another site.
Encrypts the cookie with the server's session key.
Forces the cookie to be transmitted over HTTPS only.
Makes the cookie inaccessible to the page's JavaScript.

explanation: With SameSite=Strict, the browser does not attach the session cookie to cross-site requests, which deprives the forged request of the victim's authentication.

stem: To reliably prevent XSS when displaying data entered by users, the most dependable measure is…

choices.text: Converting all input to uppercase.
Encoding the data on output according to the HTML, attribute or JavaScript context it is inserted into.
Storing the data in an HttpOnly cookie.
Limiting the maximum length of the input field.

explanation: Context-aware output encoding neutralizes special characters at the moment they are inserted into the page, which prevents the browser from interpreting the data as code.

stem: A session fixation attack succeeds when…

choices.text: The application generates a session identifier too long to memorize.
The user chooses a password that is too weak.
The session cookie is marked both HttpOnly and Secure.
The application keeps the same session identifier before and after authentication, allowing the attacker to impose an ID they already know.

explanation: If the server does not regenerate the session identifier at login, the attacker can fix an ID they know and hijack the session once the victim has authenticated.

stem: A cookie's Secure attribute guarantees that…

choices.text: The cookie is only transmitted over encrypted HTTPS connections.
The cookie expires automatically when the browser is closed.
The cookie cannot be read by the page's JavaScript.
The cookie is only sent with same-origin requests.

explanation: The Secure flag prevents the browser from sending the cookie over an unencrypted HTTP connection, which protects it from being intercepted in the clear.

stem: Why is it inadvisable for an HTTP GET request to trigger a state-changing action (for example deleting an account)?

choices.text: A GET technically cannot carry session cookies.
A GET encrypts its parameters, preventing the server from reading them.
A GET is always cached and never reaches the server.
A GET can be triggered without the user's knowledge through an image or a link, which makes it an easy CSRF vector.

explanation: GET requests can be triggered passively by third-party content (an img tag, a link), which makes them an ideal CSRF vector if they change state.

stem: Compared with RBAC, an ABAC (Attribute-Based Access Control) model decides access…

choices.text: Exclusively from the source IP address of the request.
Only from the user's hierarchical role.
While completely ignoring the identity of the requester.
By evaluating attributes of the user, of the resource and of the context rather than a simple assigned role.

explanation: ABAC bases its decisions on combined attributes (role, resource sensitivity, time of day, location), offering finer granularity than RBAC's role alone.

stem: Setting up a session idle timeout is mainly intended to…

choices.text: Reduce the window during which a session left open can be hijacked.
Speed up page loading for the user.
Automatically encrypt session data on the server side.
Prevent the user from choosing a weak password.

explanation: An idle timeout invalidates an inactive session after a delay, which limits how long an unattended workstation remains exploitable.

stem: The principle of least privilege recommends…

choices.text: Giving everyone maximum rights to avoid being blocked
Granting only the rights strictly necessary for a task
Disabling audit logs
Sharing a single administrator account

explanation: Fewer rights = a smaller attack surface and a reduced impact if a compromise occurs.

stem: In a CSP policy, using a nonce on script tags makes it possible to…

choices.text: Prevent the browser from caching the scripts.
Allow only the scripts carrying the expected random value to run, blocking injected scripts.
Force scripts to be loaded over HTTPS only.
Encrypt the content of the scripts before they are sent to the browser.

explanation: The nonce is a random value regenerated for every response; only the scripts carrying it are executed, which neutralizes scripts injected by an attacker who does not know it.

stem: A scheduled job (cron) that only needs to read a log directory should run…

choices.text: Under the account of the administrator who was logged in when it was created.
Under an account shared by all the services on the machine.
Under a dedicated unprivileged account holding read access to that directory only.
Under the root account, to avoid any permission problems.

explanation: Applying least privilege calls for a dedicated account limited to reading the directory concerned, so that a compromise of the job does not open up broader access.

stem: Even if the session cookie is protected by HttpOnly, an XSS flaw remains dangerous because the attacker can…
