choices.text: Act in the victim's context by issuing authenticated requests from their browser.
Modify the RBAC permissions in the database without any request at all.
Bypass the connection's TLS encryption.
Directly decrypt the password stored on the server.

explanation: HttpOnly only prevents the cookie from being read; the injected script runs inside the victim's session and can perform authenticated actions on their behalf.

stem: The X-Frame-Options: DENY header protects against clickjacking by…

choices.text: Forcing all of the page's requests to use HTTPS.
Forbidding the page from being displayed inside an iframe.
Blocking the execution of the page's inline scripts.
Removing the third-party cookies associated with the page.

explanation: By preventing the page from being framed, X-Frame-Options: DENY denies the attacker the invisible overlay that clickjacking requires.

stem: Which statement correctly distinguishes sanitization from validation of an input?

choices.text: Validation modifies the input, whereas sanitization merely rejects it.
The two are strict synonyms, with no difference in behavior.
Sanitization applies to passwords and validation to files.
Validation accepts or rejects the input, whereas sanitization modifies the input to make it safe.

explanation: Validating means deciding whether the input is acceptable (accept or reject), whereas sanitizing transforms the input by stripping or escaping characters to neutralize its danger.

stem: After a successful authentication, regenerating the session identifier is recommended in order to…

choices.text: Encrypt the connection between the client and the server.
Reduce the size of the cookies stored by the browser.
Prevent session fixation by invalidating any identifier established before login.
Speed up password verification.

explanation: Issuing a new identifier at login guarantees that an ID an attacker may have fixed before authentication becomes unusable.

stem: A standard user who accesses the data of another user holding the same level of rights is carrying out…

choices.text: A horizontal privilege escalation.
A denial-of-service attack.
A vertical privilege escalation.
A blind SQL injection.

explanation: Accessing the resources of a peer with the same level of rights is horizontal escalation, as opposed to vertical escalation, which targets higher privileges.

stem: Hiding an "Administration" button in the interface without checking the role on the server side is an access control flaw because…

choices.text: The browser always redisplays hidden elements after a reload.
Access control must be enforced on the server side, since the user can call the protected endpoint directly.
Hidden buttons are automatically indexed by search engines.
Hiding elements with CSS needlessly slows down page rendering.

explanation: Client-side security can be bypassed; without an authorization check on the server, a user can invoke the administration function directly.

stem: Separation of duties requires that, for a sensitive operation such as a large transfer…

choices.text: The account used is always the administrator account.
Its creation and its approval are carried out by two different people.
The operation is systematically encrypted end to end.
A single person holds all the rights, so things move faster.

explanation: Separation of duties splits a critical action across several actors so that no single person can fraudulently carry it through alone.

stem: Against which type of attack is a well-configured Content-Security-Policy most effective?

choices.text: SQL injection into the database.
Script injection (XSS).
Theft of the backups on the file server.
Distributed denial of service (DDoS).

explanation: By restricting the allowed script sources and blocking unapproved inline code, CSP mainly reduces the execution of XSS payloads.

stem: The main difference between a stored XSS and a reflected XSS is that…

choices.text: Stored XSS only runs in the DOM and never reaches the server.
Reflected XSS only targets cookies and stored XSS only localStorage.
Stored XSS saves the malicious payload on the server and serves it to every visitor of the page, whereas reflected XSS returns the payload supplied in the current request.
Reflected XSS persists in the database while stored XSS lives only in the URL.

explanation: Stored XSS persists the payload on the server and displays it to all the users concerned, whereas reflected XSS sends it straight back from the current request.

stem: The "deny by default" principle in access control means that…

choices.text: Any action that is not explicitly forbidden is allowed.
Any action that is not explicitly allowed is denied.
Administrators are exempt from any access check.
Access is granted as long as the user is authenticated.

explanation: With deny by default, the absence of an explicit authorization results in a denial, which avoids accidental access caused by a forgotten rule.

stem: An anti-CSRF token protects a sensitive action because…

choices.text: It blocks any request coming from a browser other than the original one.
It is unpredictable and tied to the session, so a third-party site cannot guess it in order to forge a valid request.
It encrypts the request body to prevent it from being intercepted on the network.
It checks that the user's password is correct on every request.

explanation: The CSRF token is a secret, unpredictable value tied to the session that the attacking site cannot reproduce, which invalidates the forged request.

stem: A web application connects to its database with an account holding DROP TABLE and GRANT rights even though it only reads and writes records; which principle is violated?

choices.text: The principle of non-repudiation.
The principle of least privilege.
The principle of defense in depth.
The principle of separation of duties.

explanation: The account holds rights far beyond those required for its function, which violates the principle of least privilege.

stem: In an RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) model, permissions are assigned…

choices.text: According to the classification level of the document being accessed alone.
Directly to each individual user, with no notion of role.
According to contextual attributes such as the time of day or the location.
To roles, and users obtain those permissions by being assigned one or more roles.

explanation: RBAC attaches permissions to roles, and users inherit the permissions through the roles they are assigned.

stem: When validating user input, why is an allowlist approach generally preferred over a denylist?

choices.text: It automatically encrypts the input before it is processed.
It runs faster because the list is always shorter.
It makes any output encoding of the data unnecessary.
It accepts only what is explicitly known to be valid, which also rejects unanticipated malicious cases.

explanation: An allowlist defines precisely what is permitted and rejects everything else, including the malicious payloads a denylist would not have anticipated.

stem: The HttpOnly attribute set on a session cookie is mainly used to…

choices.text: Restrict the cookie to same-site requests only.
Force the cookie to be sent over HTTPS connections only.
Prevent access to the cookie from JavaScript, limiting its theft in the event of XSS.
Encrypt the cookie value on the client side.

explanation: HttpOnly makes the cookie inaccessible to document.cookie, which prevents an XSS script from exfiltrating it.

stem: The main purpose of a Content-Security-Policy (CSP) header is to…

choices.text: Force the browser to use HTTPS for all its future connections to the site.
Prevent third-party cookies from being stored in the browser.
Restrict the sources from which the browser may load scripts, styles and other resources.
Digitally sign every HTTP response from the server.

explanation: CSP declares the allowed origins for each type of resource, greatly reducing the impact of script injection (XSS).

stem: During a TLS connection, asymmetric cryptography is mainly used to…

choices.text: Compress the data
Encrypt all of the session's data
Authenticate the user with a password
Securely exchange a symmetric session key and authenticate the server

explanation: Asymmetric cryptography establishes trust and negotiates a key; the rest of the session uses symmetric cryptography (which is faster).

stem: Non-repudiation of the origin of a message relies on…

choices.text: A digital signature produced with the sender's private key
Symmetric encryption of the message
A plain SHA-256 digest
The use of a VPN tunnel

explanation: Only the holder of the private key can sign, which prevents the sender from later denying authorship.

stem: To send a confidential message to Alice, Bob encrypts it with…

choices.text: His own private key
Alice's public key
His own public key
Alice's private key

explanation: Encrypted with Alice's public key, the message can only be decrypted with the private key that she alone holds.

stem: During the TLS handshake, the server proves its identity to the client by…

choices.text: Sending its password to the client
Presenting its certificate and proving that it holds the corresponding private key
Sending its private key to the client
Opening a dedicated TCP port

explanation: The certificate binds the identity to a public key, and proof of possession of the associated private key authenticates the server.

stem: In asymmetric encryption, what is encrypted with a public key can only be decrypted with…

choices.text: The corresponding private key
The same public key
Any key in the domain
A symmetric session key

explanation: The public and private keys form a pair, and only the other member of the pair can reverse the operation.

stem: Technically, producing a digital signature amounts to…

choices.text: Encrypting the message's hash with the sender's private key
Encrypting the message with a shared symmetric key
Compressing the message and then encoding it in base64
Encrypting the entire message with the recipient's public key

explanation: The message digest is signed with the private key, and anyone can verify it with the corresponding public key.

stem: A browser warns about a self-signed certificate mainly because…

choices.text: It is only compatible with TLS 1.3
Its key is too long to be valid
It encrypts data too weakly
No recognized certificate authority vouches for its identity

explanation: Without a signature from a trusted CA, nothing guarantees that the certificate really belongs to the site it claims to represent.

stem: The OCSP protocol allows a client to…

choices.text: Negotiate the cipher suite of the handshake
Compress the data exchanged over TLS
Check in real time whether a certificate has been revoked
Generate an ephemeral key pair

explanation: OCSP queries the authority for the revocation status of a certificate, a lighter alternative to CRL lists.

stem: The confidentiality of data transmitted over the network is achieved above all through…

choices.text: Access logging
Digital signatures
Encryption
Hashing

explanation: Encryption makes the data unreadable to anyone who does not hold the key, which guarantees confidentiality.

stem: The Diffie-Hellman key exchange allows two parties to…

choices.text: Mutually sign a document
Revoke a compromised certificate
Establish a shared secret key without ever transmitting it in the clear
Hash a password securely

explanation: Diffie-Hellman derives a shared secret from public exchanges, without the key itself ever travelling over the wire.

stem: Unlike a plain hash, an HMAC cannot be recomputed by an attacker without knowing…

choices.text: The shared secret key
The server's IP address
The hash algorithm in use
The exact size of the message

explanation: The algorithm is public, but without the secret key it is impossible to forge a valid HMAC.

stem: In the CIA triad of information security, the "I" stands for…

choices.text: Internet
Isolation
Integrity: data is not altered in an unauthorized way
Identification

explanation: CIA = Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability.

stem: The best practice for storing passwords server-side is to…

choices.text: Hash them with a slow, salted function such as bcrypt or Argon2
Store them in plaintext in a database with restricted access
Encode them in base64
Encrypt them with AES using a key stored alongside them

explanation: A slow, salted hash avoids reversible storage and holds up well against offline attacks if the database leaks.

stem: The main purpose of a VLAN is to…

choices.text: Filter malicious URLs
Logically segment a network into separate broadcast domains
Encrypt traffic between two sites
Translate private IP addresses into public ones

explanation: A VLAN isolates traffic into logical subnetworks on the same physical infrastructure, improving both security and organization.

stem: The main role of NAT (Network Address Translation) is to…

choices.text: Encrypt IP headers
Authenticate the hosts on the network
Isolate VLANs from one another
Map private IP addresses to a shared public address

explanation: NAT translates internal private addresses to one (or a few) public addresses for Internet access.

stem: A reverse proxy placed in front of web servers makes it possible to…

choices.text: Issue root certificates
Receive client requests and distribute them while hiding the back-end servers
Replace password hashing
Assign IP addresses via DHCP

explanation: The reverse proxy acts as a single entry point, balances the load and conceals the internal server architecture.

stem: On Linux, running `chmod 640` on a file grants…

choices.text: Read/write to the owner, read-only to the group, nothing to others
Full permissions to every user
Execute permission to others only
Read-only to the owner alone

explanation: In octal, 6 means rw- for the owner, 4 means r-- for the group and 0 means no permissions for others.

stem: TLS is the direct successor to which protocol, now obsolete?

choices.text: SSH
IPsec
Kerberos
SSL

explanation: TLS replaced SSL, whose last versions are considered insecure and have been deprecated.

stem: The main problem with symmetric cryptography, which asymmetric cryptography solves in TLS, is…

choices.text: Securely distributing the shared key between the parties
Key lengths that are far too short
The inability to encrypt large files
Excessively slow encryption

explanation: Asymmetric cryptography allows the symmetric key to be exchanged or negotiated without having to send it in the clear beforehand.

stem: A cryptographic hash function must be…

choices.text: One-way and collision resistant
Of variable output length depending on the input
Easily reversible with the right key
Encrypted with the server's public key

explanation: A good hash function makes it infeasible to recover the input or to find two inputs producing the same digest.

stem: For a single certificate to cover both `example.com` and `www.example.com`, you fill in the field…

choices.text: SAN (Subject Alternative Name)
Serial Number
CRL
OCSP

explanation: The SAN field lists every domain name (and sometimes IP address) covered by the same certificate.

stem: Compared with plain authentication, non-repudiation additionally guarantees that…

choices.text: The message is delivered without delay
The author cannot later deny being behind the action
The data stays encrypted end to end
The session key changes on every connection

explanation: Non-repudiation provides defensible proof of origin, preventing the author from disputing the action afterwards.

stem: After the TLS handshake, application data is encrypted symmetrically rather than asymmetrically because…

choices.text: Symmetric cryptography automatically authenticates the server
Asymmetric cryptography cannot encrypt binary data
Symmetric cryptography always uses a shorter, and therefore safer, key
Symmetric cryptography is far faster and computationally lighter

explanation: Asymmetric cryptography is used to negotiate a session key, but the data stream itself is encrypted symmetrically for performance reasons.

stem: Applying the principle of least privilege contributes to defense in depth by…

choices.text: Granting each account only the permissions it strictly needs
Doubling the available bandwidth
Deploying several antivirus products in parallel
Encrypting all communications

explanation: Limiting each account's permissions reduces the attack surface and contains the impact of a compromise, one layer among others.

stem: An X.509 digital certificate primarily binds…

choices.text: A hash algorithm to a network port
A password to a user account
An identity (a domain name, for example) to a public key
A private key to a MAC address

explanation: The certificate attests that a public key really belongs to the stated identity, backed by the signature of a certificate authority.

stem: In a TLS chain of trust, trust ultimately rests on…

choices.text: The server's public IP address
A certificate authority root certificate present in the trust store
The certificate of the web server being visited
The private key stored in the browser

explanation: Validation walks up the chain to a root certificate that is preinstalled and considered trustworthy by the system.

stem: A distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attack mainly targets…

choices.text: Data integrity
Data confidentiality
Non-repudiation
Service availability

explanation: A DDoS saturates resources to make the service unreachable, which directly attacks the availability leg of the CIA triad.

stem: bcrypt is preferred over plain SHA-256 for storing passwords because it…

choices.text: Always produces a shorter digest
Is reversible when a password is forgotten
Does away with salting entirely
Is deliberately slow and offers an adjustable cost factor

explanation: Its tunable slowness dramatically slows down brute-force attacks, unlike fast hash functions.

stem: A unique salt per password is mainly used to…

choices.text: Prevent two identical passwords from producing the same hash and defeat rainbow tables
Speed up hash computation
Make hashing reversible
Reduce the size of the database

explanation: The salt makes each digest unique and neutralizes precomputed tables such as rainbow tables.

stem: An HMAC provides both the integrity and the authenticity of a message because it…

choices.text: Combines a hash function with a shared secret key
Relies on the recipient's public key
Requires a valid X.509 certificate
Encrypts the entire message content

explanation: Only holders of the secret key can produce or verify the HMAC, which proves both origin and integrity.

stem: In an Active Directory domain, Kerberos authentication mainly relies on…

choices.text: Sending the password in the clear on every access
X.509 certificates only
Tickets issued by a KDC to authenticate to services
The MAC address used as an identity

explanation: The KDC (Key Distribution Center) issues tickets (a TGT, then service tickets); the password is not sent on every access.

stem: In a Kerberos infrastructure under Active Directory, the KDC role is performed by…

choices.text: The domain controller
The network's DHCP server
The perimeter firewall
Each client workstation on its own

explanation: Under Active Directory, every domain controller hosts the KDC that issues TGTs and service tickets.

stem: Segmenting a network into several VLANs primarily allows you to…

choices.text: Natively encrypt every Ethernet frame
Increase the physical bandwidth of the cabling
Isolate broadcast domains and partition traffic between groups of machines
Remove the need for IP addresses

explanation: VLANs split the network into separate broadcast domains, which partitions traffic and limits how far a compromise can spread.

stem: A DMZ (demilitarized zone) is mainly used to…

choices.text: Store offline backups
Host Internet-facing services while isolating them from the internal network
Speed up internal DNS resolution
Replace the domain controller

explanation: The DMZ isolates Internet-facing services from the LAN, so that a compromise of those services does not directly reach the internal network.

stem: A canary value (stack cookie) inserted by the compiler is intended to…

choices.text: Detect stack corruption before the function returns
Speed up function calls
Prevent heap fragmentation
Encrypt local variables

explanation: The canary is checked before the function returns: if an overflow has modified it, the program aborts instead of using a corrupted return address.

stem: The timestamps included in Kerberos exchanges mainly serve to…

choices.text: Encrypt the user's password
Synchronize the routers' clocks
Assign IP addresses
Limit replay attacks by enforcing a short time window

explanation: Timestamps, combined with a tight clock skew tolerance, prevent a captured message from being replayed later by an attacker.

stem: Compared with NTLM, Kerberos notably brings…

choices.text: Mutual authentication and the use of time-limited tickets rather than a simple challenge-response
Transmission of the password in the clear
The inability to operate within a domain
Complete abandonment of any cryptography

explanation: Kerberos lets the client and the service authenticate each other and relies on tickets with a limited lifetime, unlike NTLM challenge-response.

stem: When several GPOs apply, the default processing order follows the sequence…

choices.text: Local, then Site, then Domain, then Organizational Unit (OU)
A random order redefined at every startup
Organizational Unit, then Domain, then Site, then Local
Only the Local GPO, all the others being ignored

explanation: The LSDOU order (Local, Site, Domain, OU) means the GPO processed last, the OU one, wins in case of conflict.

stem: A signature-based IDS differs from a behavior-based (anomaly) IDS in that it…

choices.text: Learns normal traffic on its own without any rule base
Never generates false positives
Systematically blocks the traffic
Detects known attack patterns but struggles against novel (zero-day) threats

explanation: A signature-based IDS reliably recognizes already catalogued attacks but stays blind to unknown threats that have no signature yet.

stem: The main benefit of centralizing logs from many devices in a SIEM is to…

choices.text: Encrypt network traffic in transit
Correlate events from varied sources to detect attacks that span systems
Replace database backups
Reduce the power consumption of the servers

explanation: The SIEM gathers logs from heterogeneous sources and correlates them, revealing attack scenarios that are invisible on any single device.

stem: As part of a hardening effort, disabling unnecessary system services mainly aims to…

choices.text: Remove the need for security patches
Automatically encrypt user files
Reduce the attack surface exposed on the machine
Increase hard drive speed

explanation: Every running service is a potential entry point: disabling the ones you do not need shrinks the attack surface accordingly.

stem: A buffer overflow exploits…

choices.text: Writing data beyond the allocated memory, potentially hijacking execution
An open network port
An expired certificate
A reused password

explanation: Writing past the bounds of a buffer can overwrite control data (such as the return address) and lead to code execution.

stem: A heap overflow differs from a stack overflow in that it…

choices.text: Affects constant variables only
Can never lead to code execution
Corrupts the management structures of dynamically allocated memory rather than the return address
Occurs only on 16-bit systems

explanation: A heap overflow corrupts the heap's dynamic allocation metadata, whereas a stack overflow mainly targets the return address on the stack.

stem: In Active Directory, an SPN (Service Principal Name) is used to…

choices.text: Assign a static IP address to a server
Encrypt GPOs
Replace the domain's DNS name
Uniquely identify a service instance so that Kerberos authentication is possible

explanation: The SPN ties a service instance to an account, letting the client request the right Kerberos ticket for that service from the KDC.

stem: A pass-the-hash attack exploits the fact that NTLM…

choices.text: Enforces hourly key rotation
Authenticates from the password hash, which can be reused without knowing the cleartext password
Always transmits a verifiable certificate
Encrypts the hash with the server's public key

explanation: Since NTLM authenticates from the hash, an attacker who obtains that hash can log in without ever having the cleartext password.

stem: DEP (Data Execution Prevention), backed by the NX bit, protects against buffer overflows by…

choices.text: Forbidding any dynamic allocation
Preventing code execution in memory regions meant for data
Deliberately slowing down vulnerable functions
Encrypting the stack on every function call

explanation: DEP marks data regions as non-executable, preventing the execution of shellcode injected through an overflow.

stem: To prove after the fact that a log file has not been modified, you can…

choices.text: Compute its cryptographic hash and keep it in a protected location
Compress it as a ZIP archive
Rename it with the current date
Restrict it to read-only access for the administrator

explanation: By storing the file's cryptographic hash separately, any later modification becomes detectable because the recomputed hash differs.

stem: To be able to block malicious traffic, an IPS typically has to be deployed…

choices.text: Exclusively on client machines outside the network
Inline, directly in the path of the traffic
Only on a passive mirror (SPAN) port
On a segment completely disconnected from the network

explanation: Placed inline in the traffic path, the IPS can decide in real time whether to forward or drop each packet.

stem: In an Active Directory domain, the account and authentication database is hosted by…

choices.text: The client machines that are members of the domain
The domain controllers
The print server
The layer 2 switches

explanation: Domain controllers host the AD database (NTDS.dit), which holds the accounts and handles authentication.

stem: On a link between two switches carrying several VLANs, you use a trunk port, which…

choices.text: Carries only one VLAN at a time
Encrypts the frames with AES
Strips off all VLAN tagging
Tags frames with an 802.1Q tag to tell the VLANs apart

explanation: A trunk port adds an 802.1Q tag to each frame so the switch on the other end knows which VLAN it belongs to.

stem: ASLR (Address Space Layout Randomization) makes exploiting a buffer overflow harder by…

choices.text: Randomizing the addresses of memory regions on every execution
Checking antivirus signatures at startup
Forbidding fixed-size buffers
Encrypting the application's source code

explanation: By randomizing the location of the stack, the heap and the libraries, ASLR stops the attacker from predicting the addresses to redirect execution to.

stem: Compromise of a domain's krbtgt account is critical because it lets an attacker…

choices.text: Forge valid Kerberos tickets (golden tickets) for any account
Read the firewall's cleartext password
Physically shut down the network switches
Prevent any TLS encryption on the network

explanation: The krbtgt account's hash is used to sign TGTs; compromising it allows golden tickets to be forged that impersonate any user in the domain.

stem: In Kerberos, after a successful initial authentication, the TGT (Ticket Granting Ticket) handed to the client is issued by…

choices.text: The KDC's AS (Authentication Service)
The client itself, from its password
The target file server
The secondary domain controller, read-only only

explanation: The TGT is issued by the KDC's Authentication Service (AS) once the client's identity has been verified.

stem: A password policy (length, complexity, expiration) applied across an entire domain is generally defined through…

choices.text: A GPO linked to the domain (Default Domain Policy or equivalent)
The DHCP server
The core switch configuration
The hosts file on each machine

explanation: The domain password policy is carried by a GPO linked to the domain, such as the Default Domain Policy.

stem: A stack buffer overflow is dangerous mainly because it can…

choices.text: Push the processor frequency beyond its limits
Automatically encrypt the contents of memory
Fix segmentation faults on the fly
Overwrite a function's return address and hijack the execution flow

explanation: As it overflows, the data overwrites the return address on the stack, which lets execution be redirected to code chosen by the attacker.

stem: The NTLM authentication protocol relies on a mechanism of the type…

choices.text: Anonymous Diffie-Hellman key exchange
X.509 certificates signed by a root authority
Password sent in the clear on every connection
Challenge-response based on the password hash

explanation: NTLM authenticates the user through a challenge-response in which the response is computed from the password hash, which is never sent in the clear.

stem: In an Active Directory domain, a GPO (Group Policy Object) is mainly used to…

choices.text: Route traffic between several VLANs
Generate Kerberos tickets in place of the KDC
Encrypt communications between domain controllers
Centrally apply configuration and security settings to users and machines

explanation: A GPO pushes configuration and security settings centrally to the user and computer objects in the domain.

stem: The fundamental difference between an IDS and an IPS is that the IPS…

choices.text: Never relies on signatures
Encrypts the traffic it inspects
Works only offline on archived captures
Can actively block malicious traffic, where the IDS merely detects and alerts

explanation: The IPS acts preventively by blocking traffic identified as malicious, whereas the IDS only detects and reports it.

stem: To guarantee log integrity against an attacker who has compromised a server, the most effective measure is to…

choices.text: Delete them every night to keep their size down
Ship them in real time to a remote, append-only collection server
Keep them in the clear on the same disk as the system
Store them locally only, with write permissions for everyone

explanation: Shipping logs in real time to a remote append-only collector prevents the local attacker from altering or erasing them.

stem: When a Kerberos user wants to access a service, they present their TGT to the TGS in order to obtain…

choices.text: A root certificate from the certificate authority
A new temporary password
A dynamic IP address via DHCP
A service ticket valid for that specific service

explanation: The TGS exchanges the TGT for a service ticket specific to the requested service, which the client then presents to that service.

stem: What is the role of a SIEM?

choices.text: Managing user passwords
Replacing the firewall
Centralizing and correlating logs to detect and investigate incidents
Encrypting endpoint disks

explanation: A SIEM aggregates logs from many sources and alerts on suspicious patterns.

stem: Why roll out patches in deployment rings at scale?

choices.text: To avoid having to test patches beforehand
To remove the need for an asset inventory
To encrypt patches while they are being distributed
To catch regressions on a pilot group before the full rollout

explanation: A phased rollout exposes a pilot group first, which makes it possible to spot a patch's side effects before extending it to the whole estate.

stem: What is the primary purpose of a SOAR platform?

choices.text: Storing the company's offsite backups
Issuing certificates for the internal PKI
Filtering inbound spam on the mail system
Orchestrating and automating incident response through playbooks

explanation: SOAR chains together automated actions defined in playbooks to speed up and standardize the handling of alerts and incidents.

stem: What role does a forward proxy play in an organization's outbound traffic?

choices.text: Assigning internal IP addresses via DHCP
Filtering and logging internal endpoints' access to the Internet
Handling database replication
Distributing certificates to public web servers

explanation: An outbound proxy relays user requests to the outside, which makes it possible to filter destinations, enforce policies and keep a record of access.

stem: What is the function of a jump server (bastion host) in an administration architecture?

choices.text: Storing antivirus signatures for the estate
Generating clients' TLS session keys
Hosting the company's public web pages
Acting as a single, controlled point of access to sensitive systems

explanation: The bastion concentrates and monitors administrative connections by enforcing a single mandatory path to critical resources.

stem: Why centralize system logs on a dedicated log server?

choices.text: To preserve the evidence even if a compromised server wipes its own logs
To reduce the total disk space consumed by encryption
To avoid having to synchronize the clocks of the machines
To remove the need for administrative accounts

explanation: Shipping logs to an external collector keeps the evidence out of reach of an attacker who tries to erase local traces.

stem: In a PKI chain of trust, what is the role of an intermediate CA?

choices.text: Directly encrypting clients' application traffic
Publishing the company's password policy
Replacing the CRL with a domain allowlist
Issuing certificates on a delegated basis so the root key can stay offline

explanation: The intermediate CA signs certificates on behalf of the root, which stays offline and protected, limiting the impact of a compromise.

stem: What response capability does an EDR offer when an endpoint is compromised?

choices.text: Isolating the machine from the network while keeping agent access for investigation
Automatically reissuing the root CA's certificates
Rebuilding the routing table of the network core
Generating a CRL for the user's certificates

explanation: EDR containment (network isolation) cuts the endpoint off from the rest of the network to stop lateral spread while still letting the analyst investigate remotely through the agent.

stem: What is the purpose of the normalization (parsing) stage in a SIEM?

choices.text: Converting logs in varied formats into common fields that rules can use
Assigning an IP address to every log source
Permanently deleting the oldest events
Encrypting logs at rest on the collector

explanation: Normalization turns heterogeneous log formats into a common schema, a prerequisite for correlating and searching effectively across sources.

stem: According to the Pyramid of Pain, which type of indicator is the most costly for an attacker to work around?

choices.text: A command-and-control IP address
A malicious file hash
Their TTPs (tactics, techniques and procedures)
A domain name used for exfiltration

explanation: Changing a hash, an IP or a domain is trivial, whereas changing TTPs forces the attacker to rethink how they operate, which hurts them the most.

stem: In MITRE ATT&CK, what is the difference between a tactic and a technique?

choices.text: The tactic is the goal being pursued, the technique is the concrete means of achieving it
The tactic is a patch, the technique is a vulnerability
The tactic is an IOC, the technique is a log
The tactic concerns the network, the technique concerns encryption

explanation: A tactic answers the "why" (persistence, for example), while a technique describes the specific "how" used to get there.

stem: In a PKI, what is the role of a certificate authority (CA)?

choices.text: Signing and vouching for the authenticity of certificates (public keys)
Symmetrically encrypting all traffic
Storing users' private keys
Blocking network ports

explanation: The CA guarantees the binding between an identity and its public key through its signature.

stem: What do frameworks such as the CIS Benchmarks provide for hardening?

choices.text: A list of known malicious IP addresses
A standardized asymmetric encryption algorithm
A directory of root certificate authorities
Detailed secure configuration recommendations for each system

explanation: The CIS Benchmarks are consensus-based secure configuration guides, per operating system or piece of software, that serve as a baseline for hardening.

stem: What is the benefit of a golden image (hardened baseline image) for deployment?

choices.text: Generating the PKI's client certificates
Encrypting traffic between endpoints and the SIEM
Removing the need to apply patches
Guaranteeing a hardened, consistent baseline on every provisioned machine

explanation: The golden image provides a validated, hardened template that is deployed identically every time, ensuring a consistent configuration and reducing security drift.

stem: What is the main distinction between a SIEM and a SOAR?

choices.text: The SIEM detects and alerts, the SOAR orchestrates and automates the response
The SIEM encrypts data, the SOAR backs it up
The SIEM is an antivirus, the SOAR is a proxy
The SIEM manages certificates, the SOAR manages firewalls

explanation: The SIEM focuses on collection, correlation and detection, while the SOAR takes over to orchestrate and automate response actions.

stem: Where is a reverse proxy typically placed in an architecture?

choices.text: Inside the CA's private key
Only on the central log server
On every user endpoint to filter its browsing
In front of the application servers, on the inbound side, to receive requests from external clients

explanation: The reverse proxy sits in front of the internal servers to receive inbound traffic, which enables TLS termination, filtering and load balancing among other things.

stem: What does OCSP stapling add compared with a standard OCSP check?

choices.text: It extends the lifetime of a revoked certificate
It encrypts the server's private key before sending it
It makes a CA in the chain unnecessary
The server itself attaches a signed OCSP response, sparing the client from querying the responder

explanation: With OCSP stapling, the server presents a recent, signed OCSP response during the TLS handshake, which improves both the performance and the privacy of the check.

stem: What does threat hunting involve?

choices.text: Proactively searching for undetected threats based on hypotheses
Applying monthly patches across the estate
Issuing and revoking the PKI's certificates
Passively waiting for a SIEM alert to fire

explanation: Threat hunting is a proactive practice in which the analyst forms hypotheses and digs through telemetry to flush out threats that slipped past detection.

stem: What does UEBA behavioral analytics aim to spot?

choices.text: The bandwidth available on the Internet link
Deviations from the usual behavior of users and entities
Certificates nearing expiration in the PKI
Missing patches on servers

explanation: UEBA establishes a baseline of normal behavior in order to flag anomalies, such as a login at an unusual hour or an abnormal volume of data.

stem: On what basis is remediation prioritized in vulnerability management?

choices.text: The size of the patch file to download
The creation date of the administrator account involved
Severity (CVSS score) combined with exposure and asset value
The alphabetical order of the CVE identifiers

explanation: Prioritization follows actual risk, cross-referencing severity (CVSS), the asset's exposure and its business importance, rather than treating every flaw the same way.

stem: Why enrich a SOC alert with contextual threat intelligence?

choices.text: To automatically encrypt the logs involved
To tie an IOC to a known actor or campaign and prioritize the response
To generate a new key pair for the analyst
To drop false positives without analyzing them

explanation: Threat intelligence context links an indicator to documented adversaries or campaigns, which helps the analyst assess severity and decide on a response.

stem: Why prefer key-based authentication over password authentication on an SSH bastion?

choices.text: The key automatically grants root privileges
The key removes the need to log connections
The key resists brute-force attacks and exposes no reusable secret in cleartext
The key on its own encrypts the bastion's entire disk

explanation: An asymmetric key pair is far more resistant to brute force than a password and avoids transmitting a guessable secret, which strengthens access to the bastion.

stem: Beyond simply storing logs, what is a SIEM's distinctive function?

choices.text: Replacing the perimeter firewall
Correlating events from heterogeneous sources to detect attack patterns
Encrypting the disks of production servers
Automatically distributing patches across the estate

explanation: The SIEM aggregates and normalizes logs from varied sources, then applies correlation rules to surface attack scenarios that are invisible source by source.

stem: What is the main risk of a self-signed certificate on a user-facing service?

choices.text: No trusted CA validates it, which makes man-in-the-middle attacks easier
It encrypts traffic too strongly for clients
It forces system logging to be disabled
It prevents any later revocation of the root key

explanation: Because a self-signed certificate is not backed by any trusted authority, clients cannot verify the server's identity, which opens the door to interception.

stem: What is a CRL (Certificate Revocation List) used for in a PKI?

choices.text: Generating end users' key pairs
Publishing the list of certificates revoked before their expiration date
Assigning a unique identifier to each root authority
Encrypting traffic between client and server

explanation: The CRL is a CA-signed list enumerating certificates invalidated before their expiry, which a client downloads to check that a certificate has not been revoked.

stem: What advantage does OCSP offer over a traditional CRL?

choices.text: It checks the status of a single certificate in real time instead of downloading a full list
It automatically extends the validity of expired certificates
It removes the need for a certificate authority
It encrypts the CA's private key

explanation: OCSP queries a responder for the status of one specific certificate, avoiding the download and processing of a CRL that can be very large.

stem: What mainly sets an EDR apart from a traditional antivirus?

choices.text: The EDR works only offline with no agent installed
The EDR only compares files against a signature database
The antivirus automatically isolates compromised machines from the network
The EDR records behavioral telemetry from endpoints and enables investigation and response

explanation: An EDR continuously collects endpoint telemetry to detect suspicious behavior and provide investigation and response capabilities, whereas a traditional antivirus relies mostly on signatures.

stem: What does an IOC (Indicator of Compromise) refer to in threat intelligence?

choices.text: A service-level agreement between the SOC and the business
A score measuring the strength of an encryption algorithm
An observable artifact (hash, IP, domain) signaling a possible compromise
A password policy imposed on users

explanation: An IOC is an observable technical element, such as a file hash or a malicious IP address, that helps spot the traces of an intrusion.

stem: What does the MITRE ATT&CK framework primarily describe?

choices.text: A symmetric key exchange protocol
The tactics and techniques observed in real-world adversaries
An encrypted list of known software vulnerabilities
A network configuration standard for VLANs

explanation: ATT&CK is a knowledge base that maps the tactics (goals) and techniques (means) actually used by attackers throughout an intrusion.

stem: What is the core objective of system hardening?

choices.text: Encrypting all internal network communications
Reducing the attack surface by disabling unnecessary services, ports and accounts
Spreading the load across several servers
Increasing the frequency of full backups

explanation: Hardening consists of removing or locking down everything that is not needed (services, accounts, ports, features) in order to reduce the exploitable entry points.

stem: Certificate pinning helps protect against…

choices.text: Power outages
SMS phishing
A man-in-the-middle (MITM) attack presenting a forged certificate
Memory leaks

explanation: By accepting only an expected certificate or key, the app rejects a fraudulent certificate even if it was signed by a compromised CA.

stem: Which countermeasure on a managed switch limits ARP spoofing?

choices.text: Port mirroring to an IDS
NAT on the edge router
Enabling IPv6 on the VLAN
Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) combined with DHCP snooping

explanation: DAI validates the IP/MAC pairs in ARP frames against the binding table built by DHCP snooping and drops forged replies.

stem: What does a DNS cache poisoning attack try to achieve?

choices.text: Encrypting the zone's DNS records
Making a resolver cache a falsified name-to-IP mapping
Exhausting the firewall's NAT table
Deleting the domain's MX records

explanation: By inserting a forged answer into the resolver's cache, the attacker redirects subsequent queries to their own server.

stem: The Kaminsky DNS attack mainly exploits…

choices.text: The lack of encryption on the HTTPS channel
A buffer overflow in the BIND server
The reuse of DNSSEC keys across zones
The predictability of the transaction ID combined with a fixed source port

explanation: Guessing the 16-bit transaction ID becomes realistic when the source port is fixed, hence source port randomization as a defense.

stem: Which mechanism guarantees the authenticity and integrity of DNS responses against poisoning?

choices.text: DNSSEC, which cryptographically signs the records
Split-horizon DNS
DNS round-robin
A deliberately very short TTL

explanation: DNSSEC signs records through a chain of trust, which lets the resolver reject a forged response.

stem: What does DNS over HTTPS (DoH) protect that DNSSEC does not?

choices.text: The confidentiality of DNS queries from an on-path network observer
The integrity of the signed zone file
The availability of the root servers
The cryptographic authenticity of the zone's records

explanation: DoH encrypts the transport of queries and ensures their confidentiality, whereas DNSSEC only guarantees authenticity and encrypts nothing.

stem: What is bcrypt's cost factor (work factor) for?

choices.text: Compressing the password before hashing
Lengthening the salt to avoid collisions
Encrypting the hash with a secret key
Increasing the computational cost of each hash in order to slow down brute-force attacks

explanation: Each increment of the cost factor doubles the work required, which slows offline brute-force attacks by the same amount.

stem: Which property of scrypt makes it more resistant than bcrypt to GPU or ASIC cracking?

choices.text: It encrypts the result with AES-256
It applies ten thousand iterations of SHA-256
It is memory-hard: it requires a large amount of RAM, which is expensive to parallelize
It always produces a 512-bit hash

explanation: By demanding heavy memory usage, scrypt makes massive parallelism on GPUs or ASICs far more expensive.

stem: Why is the Argon2id variant generally recommended?

choices.text: It is the fastest variant to compute on the server side
It produces a deterministic hash with no parameters
It combines resistance to side-channel attacks and to GPU cracking
It works without a salt

explanation: Argon2id blends Argon2i (side-channel resistant) and Argon2d (GPU resistant), offering a good default trade-off.

stem: What is the difference between a salt and a pepper in password hashing?

choices.text: The salt encrypts the hash and the pepper decrypts it
The salt is secret while the pepper is public
The two are identical and interchangeable
The salt is unique per user and stored in the database; the pepper is a global secret kept outside the database

explanation: The public, per-account salt defeats rainbow tables, while the secret pepper held outside the database still protects the hashes if only the database leaks.

stem: Which historical limitation of bcrypt can weaken very long passphrases?

choices.text: It rejects any Unicode character
It ignores input bytes beyond roughly 72 characters
It truncates the salt to 8 bits
It only accepts ASCII passwords

explanation: bcrypt only takes about 72 bytes into account, so beyond that the extra characters add no entropy at all.

stem: Against an offline dictionary attack on password hashes, which measure is the most effective?

choices.text: Using a fast hash such as MD5
Shortening the passwords
Encoding the passwords in Base64
Using a slow, salted algorithm designed for passwords (bcrypt, scrypt, Argon2)

explanation: The high computational cost (and the salt) makes large-scale offline cracking far slower.

stem: What does the "alg: none" attack on a JSON Web Token (JWT) consist of?

choices.text: Replacing the token's header with XML
Encrypting the payload with a null key
Compressing the token to exceed its maximum size
Forging a token by setting the algorithm to "none" so that no signature is verified

explanation: If the server accepts alg:none, it validates a token with no signature at all, which allows any payload to be forged.

stem: The RS256 → HS256 algorithm confusion attack on a JWT exploits…

choices.text: A collision in the SHA-256 function
Storing the token in localStorage
The use of the RSA public key as the HMAC secret when the server does not pin the algorithm
The absence of the "exp" claim in the payload

explanation: If the server picks the algorithm from the header, the attacker signs with HS256 using the well-known public key and the server accepts it.

stem: Which statement about a signed JWT (JWS) is correct?

choices.text: Its payload is encrypted and unreadable without the private key
Its payload is only Base64URL-encoded, so it is readable by anyone holding the token
It can never expire
Its signature encrypts the claims

explanation: The signature provides integrity but not confidentiality: the claims remain readable, so no secret should ever be placed in them.

stem: What is the security drawback of stateless JWTs for session management?

choices.text: They cannot contain custom claims
They are hard to revoke before expiry without a blocklist mechanism
They require a dedicated database per user
They require an unencrypted cleartext cookie

explanation: Since the server keeps no state, a stolen JWT stays valid until its exp, unless a blocklist or short rotation is maintained.

stem: Which category ranked first in the OWASP Top 10 in the 2021 edition?

choices.text: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
Broken Access Control
Injection
Cryptographic Failures

explanation: In 2021, Broken Access Control became the number 1 category, with injection dropping down and XSS now folded into it.

stem: An SSRF (Server-Side Request Forgery) attack typically lets the attacker…

choices.text: Inject JavaScript into the victim's browser
Redirect DNS for the entire network
Crack the password hashes stored in the database
Make the server issue requests to internal resources that are normally unreachable

explanation: By controlling a URL used on the server side, the attacker reaches internal services through that server, such as cloud metadata or an admin interface.

stem: In API security, what does a BOLA (or IDOR) flaw refer to?

choices.text: Session theft through a stored XSS
Command injection into the server's shell
A buffer overflow in the JSON parser
Accessing another user's object by manipulating an identifier with no authorization check

explanation: Broken Object Level Authorization occurs when the API serves /objects/{id} without verifying that the caller actually owns the object.

stem: The POODLE attack takes advantage of a downgrade to…

choices.text: AES-GCM configured with too short a key
HTTP/2 used without TLS
TLS 1.3 with 0-RTT resumption
SSL 3.0, whose poorly specified CBC padding is exploitable

explanation: By forcing a fallback to SSLv3, the attacker exploits its predictable CBC padding to decrypt traffic byte by byte.

stem: Which mechanism prevents a network attacker from forcing a TLS version downgrade during the handshake?

choices.text: Insecure renegotiation
OCSP stapling
TLS compression
The TLS_FALLBACK_SCSV signal, which tells the server that a fallback attempt is under way

explanation: TLS_FALLBACK_SCSV lets the server detect that a client was forced to downgrade and refuse the connection.

stem: The FREAK and Logjam attacks are primarily based on…

choices.text: The absence of HSTS on the targeted site
A Heartbleed-style buffer overflow in OpenSSL
An MD5 collision in the certificate signature
Forcing the use of "export-grade" cipher suites with deliberately weakened keys

explanation: These downgrade attacks push the server toward legacy "export" suites (RSA or Diffie-Hellman with short keys) that can be broken in practice.

stem: Beyond standard TLS encryption, what does certificate pinning primarily protect against?

choices.text: Theft of the session cookie stored server-side
A compromised or rogue certificate authority (CA) issuing a fraudulent yet technically "valid" certificate
Volumetric denial-of-service attacks
SQL injection in the API

explanation: Pinning accepts only one specific key or fingerprint, so it rejects even a certificate signed by a trusted but fraudulent CA.

stem: Why does a TLS intercepting proxy (MITM) normally trigger a certificate warning in the browser?

choices.text: It changes the destination IP address
It presents a certificate signed by a CA that is not in the client's trust store
It compresses HTTPS traffic
It enforces TLS 1.3 instead of TLS 1.2

explanation: Without the proxy's CA installed as trusted on the host, the certificate it forges is rejected, which produces the warning.

stem: What is the main operational risk of poorly managed certificate pinning in a mobile app?

choices.text: Disabling encryption of data at rest
Slowing the TLS handshake down by several seconds
Disclosing the server's private key to every client
Making the app unusable after a certificate rotation if the embedded pin was not updated

explanation: If the certificate or key changes while the app still ships the old pin, every connection fails until an app update is released.

stem: Which best practice avoids "bricking" an app that uses pinning when a certificate is renewed?

choices.text: Pinning only the production leaf certificate
Disabling certificate chain validation
Pinning the server's IP address instead of the public key
Also pinning a backup key (backup pin) that has not been deployed yet

explanation: Pinning a backup key ahead of time makes it possible to switch over without breaking the app when the current certificate is replaced.

stem: What does an SSL stripping attack consist of?

choices.text: The attacker floods the server with incomplete TLS handshakes
From a MITM position, the attacker forces the victim to communicate over cleartext HTTP instead of HTTPS
The attacker replaces the password hashing algorithm
The attacker breaks the server's RSA private key in real time

explanation: The malicious proxy intercepts the initial redirect and keeps the victim on HTTP while talking to the server over HTTPS.

stem: Which HTTP header effectively prevents SSL stripping on subsequent visits to a site?

choices.text: Set-Cookie with the Secure attribute
Content-Security-Policy
Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS)
X-Frame-Options

explanation: HSTS forces the browser to open only HTTPS connections to the domain, preventing any fallback to cleartext HTTP.

stem: Why is the ARP protocol vulnerable to spoofing on a local network?

choices.text: It provides no authentication of ARP replies
It relies on TCP without sequence numbers
It encrypts MAC addresses with a key that is too short
It depends on DNS to resolve addresses

explanation: Any host can send a forged ARP reply, which is accepted without verification, allowing traffic to be redirected.

stem: What is the goal of an attacker who poisons the ARP cache of both the victim and the gateway?

choices.text: To get into a man-in-the-middle (MITM) position and intercept their traffic
To rewrite the router's firmware
To guess the WPA2 Wi-Fi network key
To wipe the DNS server's routing table

explanation: By binding their own MAC address to both IP addresses, the attacker routes the traffic through themselves and can eavesdrop on it or tamper with it.

stem: A "pass-the-hash" attack consists of…

choices.text: Injecting SQL code
Brute-forcing the cleartext password
Reusing a password's hash (NTLM hash) to authenticate without knowing the password itself
Breaking a session's TLS encryption

explanation: Under NTLM the hash is enough to authenticate: stealing the hash is equivalent to stealing the password.

stem: Which of these measures directly makes it harder to extract secrets from LSASS?

choices.text: Hiding the last logged-on user name.
Enabling Credential Guard or running LSASS as a Protected Process Light (RunAsPPL).
Renaming the built-in administrator account.
Increasing the minimum password length.

explanation: Credential Guard and PPL mode isolate LSASS memory, preventing it from being read directly by a tool such as Mimikatz.

stem: Forging a golden ticket requires above all…

choices.text: The session key of a valid TGS ticket.
The hash of the domain's krbtgt account.
A certificate issued by the internal PKI.
The local administrator's cleartext password.

explanation: A golden ticket is a forged TGT signed with the krbtgt key, so its hash is essential.

stem: A silver ticket is a…

choices.text: Delegation ticket allowing any user to be impersonated.
Forged TGT granting access to the entire domain.
Anonymous authentication ticket accepted by every service.
Forged TGS for one specific service, built from that service account's hash.

explanation: A silver ticket is a TGS crafted with the targeted service's key, granting access limited to that single service.

stem: Compared with a golden ticket, a silver ticket…

choices.text: Affects only one service and does not require the krbtgt hash.
Necessarily requires DCSync replication rights.
Grants broader access because it forges a TGT.
Is validated by the KDC on every use.

explanation: Unlike a golden ticket, a silver ticket is limited to one service, is forged with that service's key and is never presented to the KDC.

stem: The danger of a server configured with unconstrained delegation is that…

choices.text: It is forced to encrypt all of its traffic with RC4.
It exposes the local SAM database to anonymous reads.
It automatically disables Kerberos pre-authentication.
The TGTs of users who authenticate to it are cached there and can be extracted.

explanation: With unconstrained delegation, the server receives and stores users' TGTs, which an attacker present on the machine can extract.

stem: Constrained delegation limits delegation…

choices.text: To a defined list of services (SPNs) that the account is allowed to target.
To the domain controllers of the local site only.
To accounts whose password is longer than 25 characters.
To a maximum lifetime of five minutes per ticket.

explanation: Constrained delegation restricts impersonation to the SPNs explicitly listed on the account through msDS-AllowedToDelegateTo.

stem: Resource-based constrained delegation (RBCD) is abused when an attacker can…

choices.text: Read the SPN of any service account.
Write the target resource's msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity attribute.
Disable SMB signing on the target share.
Change the domain controller's system time.

explanation: By writing that attribute on the resource, the attacker authorizes themselves to act on behalf of other identities and takes control of the service.

stem: PsExec performs lateral movement mainly by…

choices.text: Creating a remote service over SMB (the ADMIN$ share) to execute code.
Exploiting a Linux kernel flaw over SSH.
Injecting JavaScript into the victim's browser.
Modifying the domain's DNS records.

explanation: PsExec drops an executable on the ADMIN$ share and creates a remote service to run code on the target.

stem: Which of these mechanisms is commonly used for remote lateral movement on Windows?

choices.text: The Windows Search indexing service.
The NTP clock synchronization protocol.
WMI or WinRM (port 5985) to run commands remotely.
The SNMP protocol in read-only mode.

explanation: WMI and WinRM are remote administration channels routinely abused to execute commands on another machine.

stem: The overpass-the-hash (pass-the-key) attack consists of…

choices.text: Brute-forcing an NTLM hash back into a cleartext password.
Using the NT hash to obtain a genuine Kerberos TGT.
Relaying an NTLM authentication to an LDAP server.
Replaying an expired TGS ticket against the service.

explanation: Overpass-the-hash turns an NT hash into a legitimate Kerberos TGT, which then allows the attacker to operate over Kerberos.

stem: "Kerberoasting" aims to…

choices.text: Take down the domain's KDC
Obtain service tickets (TGS) in order to crack service account passwords offline
Encrypt a server's files
Spoof an IP address

explanation: The TGS is encrypted with the service account's hash: the attacker requests it and then cracks it offline.

stem: The DCSync attack allows an account holding replication rights to…

choices.text: Wipe the domain's security event logs.
Ask the domain controller to replicate secrets, including the krbtgt hash.
Create a new trusted Active Directory forest.
Remotely reboot every domain controller.

explanation: DCSync impersonates a domain controller and triggers replication to retrieve hashes, including the krbtgt one.

stem: Potato-style attacks (JuicyPotato, PrintSpoofer) often exploit…

choices.text: A buffer overflow in the graphics driver.
The SeImpersonatePrivilege privilege, to elevate a service to SYSTEM.
A blank password on the guest account.
A read-only registry key accessible to everyone.

explanation: These attacks abuse SeImpersonatePrivilege to impersonate a SYSTEM token and escalate privileges.

stem: The best protection against Kerberoasting for a service account is…

choices.text: Disabling Kerberos pre-authentication.
Adding the account to the Backup Operators group.
A long, random password, ideally through a gMSA (Group Managed Service Account).
Enabling RC4 encryption on the account.

explanation: A long, random secret managed by a gMSA makes offline cracking of the TGS practically impossible.

stem: The pass-the-ticket attack consists of…

choices.text: Injecting a stolen Kerberos ticket (TGT or TGS) into one's own session to usurp access.
Replacing the krbtgt hash with a known value.
Forcing the KDC to issue tickets without encryption.
Guessing an account's password from its SPN.

explanation: Pass-the-ticket re-injects a stolen ticket into the current session in order to inherit the victim's access.

stem: An NTLM relay attack typically succeeds when…

choices.text: The domain controller uses Kerberos exclusively.
All traffic is fully encrypted with IPsec.
Every account has the smart card required option enabled.
SMB (or LDAP) signing is not enforced, making it possible to relay the authentication to another service.

explanation: Without enforced signing, the attacker relays the victim's NTLM authentication to a third-party service that accepts it.

stem: Which right on the domain object is decisive for carrying out a DCSync?

choices.text: The log on as a service privilege.
Read access to the SYSVOL share.
The Replicating Directory Changes All extended right (DS-Replication-Get-Changes-All).
The right to modify local group membership.

explanation: That replication right authorizes requesting the domain's secrets and is what makes DCSync possible.

stem: A typical characteristic of a forged golden ticket is…

choices.text: An abnormal lifetime (10 years, for example) because the attacker sets the ticket fields themselves.
Mandatory online validation against two domain controllers.
AES-256 encryption systematically enforced by the KDC.
The impossibility of using it outside the host where it was created.

explanation: Since the attacker controls the forgery, they set unrealistic lifetimes, a classic indicator of a golden ticket.

stem: In Active Directory, an SPN (Service Principal Name) is used to…

choices.text: Encrypt traffic between two workstations.
Assign a static IP address to a server.
Store a user's password hash.
Map a service instance to an account, so that Kerberos can issue the right ticket.

explanation: The SPN ties a service to its account so the KDC knows which key to use to encrypt the TGS.

stem: Why do attackers often favor RC4 encryption when Kerberoasting?

choices.text: An RC4-encrypted TGS is derived directly from the NT hash, which is faster to crack than an AES key.
RC4 prevents the domain controller from logging the request.
RC4 makes it possible to forge the ticket without any hash at all.
RC4 makes the ticket valid across every domain in the forest.

explanation: With RC4, the TGS key is the account's NT hash, which speeds up cracking compared with AES.

stem: Reusing the same local administrator password across an entire estate makes lateral movement easier because…

choices.text: Once obtained, that account's hash opens every identically configured machine through pass-the-hash.
It turns every workstation into a domain controller.
It forces secrets to be replicated to the attacker.
It disables Kerberos across the whole domain.

explanation: An identical local password everywhere means a single stolen hash is enough to pivot across the whole estate via pass-the-hash.

stem: The pass-the-hash attack exploits the fact that…

choices.text: Kerberos sends passwords in cleartext over the network.
An NTLM hash can be reversed within seconds to recover the password.
The NTLM protocol authenticates from the password hash, without needing the cleartext password.
The hash changes on every authentication, which fools the server.

explanation: NTLM proves identity through knowledge of the hash alone, so a stolen hash is enough to authenticate without ever cracking the password.

stem: LAPS (Local Administrator Password Solution) reduces lateral movement by…

choices.text: Giving each machine a unique local administrator password that is rotated regularly.
Enforcing smart card usage for all accounts.
Removing the NTLM protocol from the network.
Encrypting the entire system disk.

explanation: LAPS gives every host a distinct local administrator password, breaking the password reuse that enables lateral movement.

stem: In a pass-the-hash attack on Windows, the attacker generally reuses…

choices.text: The TLS session key negotiated with the domain controller.
The SHA-256 fingerprint of the SAM file.
The NT (NTLM) hash of the compromised account.
The machine certificate stored in the local store.

explanation: Pass-the-hash replays the account's NT hash, which stands as proof of identity in NTLM.

stem: Kerberoasting primarily targets…

choices.text: Anonymous guest accounts in the domain.
Service accounts that have an SPN (Service Principal Name).
Local accounts that are not joined to the domain.
Computer accounts that have been disabled for a long time.

explanation: Only accounts carrying an SPN can have a TGS issued in their name, the raw material of Kerberoasting.

stem: During Kerberoasting, the attacker requests and then cracks offline…

choices.text: A service ticket (TGS) encrypted with the service account's hash.
An NTLMv2 hash captured during a challenge-response exchange.
The NTDS.dit database replicated from the domain controller.
The TGT ticket encrypted with the krbtgt key.

explanation: The TGS is encrypted with the service account's key, which lets the attacker test passwords offline until the account's password is found.

stem: AS-REP roasting is only possible against accounts…

choices.text: Configured for constrained delegation.
For which Kerberos pre-authentication is disabled (DONT_REQUIRE_PREAUTH).
That are members of the Enterprise Admins group.
Whose password never expires.

explanation: Without pre-authentication, the KDC returns an AS-REP encrypted with the user's key, which can be attacked offline.

stem: In AS-REP roasting, the element cracked offline is…

choices.text: The LM hash stored in the local SAM database.
The shared secret between two domain controllers.
The service ticket of an internal web application.
The portion of the AS-REP encrypted with the key derived from the user's password.

explanation: The AS-REP contains a block encrypted with the user's key, which the attacker cracks offline to recover the password.

stem: For an attacker using Mimikatz, the value of the LSASS process is that it…

choices.text: Stores every domain password in cleartext on disk.
Holds the private key of the root certificate authority.
Logs the keystrokes of the current session.
Keeps authentication secrets in memory (hashes, tickets, sometimes passwords).

explanation: LSASS keeps the secrets of open sessions in memory, which Mimikatz reads to retrieve hashes and tickets.

stem: In digital forensics, according to the order of volatility, what should be collected first?

choices.text: Volatile memory (RAM) and network state, which are the most volatile
The files on the hard drive
Offline backups
Logs that have already been archived

explanation: You capture whatever disappears fastest (RAM, connections) before the persistent data.

stem: Why is the ROP technique necessary when memory is marked non-executable?

choices.text: It automatically grows the size of the stack
It reuses instruction sequences already present in executable memory instead of injecting code
It encrypts the shellcode to make it invisible to antivirus
It disables the antivirus before the payload runs

explanation: ROP chains together "gadgets" already mapped in executable regions, bypassing the ban on executing injected data.

stem: What does a "gadget" used in a ROP chain typically end with?

choices.text: An exit system call that stops the program
A ret instruction that chains into the next gadget
An unconditional jump to address 0x00000000
A nop padding instruction

explanation: Each gadget ends with a ret (or equivalent) that pops the address of the next gadget, building the chain.

stem: What is the principle behind DEP protection (the NX bit)?

choices.text: Marking certain memory pages as non-executable to prevent data from being executed
Encrypting the contents of the stack on every function call
Randomizing the base address of loaded libraries
Checking a sentinel value before each function returns

explanation: DEP/NX makes data regions (stack, heap) non-executable, blocking direct execution of injected shellcode.

stem: What does ASLR randomize on each run of a program?

choices.text: The base addresses of the stack, the heap and the loaded libraries
The contents of each function's local variables
File permissions on disk
The execution order of the program's instructions

explanation: ASLR changes the base addresses of the segments on every launch, making an exploit's targets unpredictable.

stem: Which weakness often allows an attacker to bypass ASLR?

choices.text: A user password that is too short
A weak TLS encryption key
An information leak (memory leak) revealing a real memory address
An expired server certificate

explanation: Leaking an address lets the attacker recompute a module's base and defeat ASLR's randomization.

stem: Why is ASLR generally weaker on a 32-bit architecture than on a 64-bit one?

choices.text: The 32-bit architecture has no execution stack
The 32-bit architecture does not support the NX bit
Registers are encrypted only on 64-bit systems
The reduced address entropy makes brute-forcing addresses feasible

explanation: On 32-bit systems, the small number of randomized bits allows addresses to be brute-forced in a reasonable amount of time.

stem: What is the purpose of a canary value (stack canary / stack cookie)?

choices.text: Compressing the binary to reduce its size
Randomizing memory addresses on every run
Encrypting outbound network communications
Detecting that the stack has been overwritten before the function returns

explanation: A sentinel value placed before the return address is checked on exit: if it has changed, a stack overflow has been detected.

stem: What does a disassembler produce when run on a compiled binary?

choices.text: The assembly language code corresponding to the machine instructions
The application's network architecture diagram
The original C source code, commented and indented
The list of database users

explanation: A disassembler translates machine opcodes into assembly mnemonics, without recovering the original source.

stem: What characterizes static analysis of a malicious binary?

choices.text: Running it in a sandbox to observe its behavior
Examining the code and its structure without ever executing it
Automatically submitting the file to a cloud antivirus
Capturing the network traffic it generates while running

explanation: Static analysis studies the binary (strings, imports, disassembly) without launching it, unlike dynamic analysis.

stem: What does extracting the strings from a malware sample frequently reveal?

choices.text: URLs, IP addresses, or domain names of potential command-and-control (C2) servers
The private key of the attacker's server
The complete physical topology of the targeted network
The root password of the victim machine

explanation: Strings often expose indicators such as domains, IPs, or paths that reveal the C2 infrastructure.

stem: Return-Oriented Programming (ROP) is mainly used to…

choices.text: Scan the ports of a machine
Encrypt a malware payload
Bypass DEP/NX protection by chaining fragments of code already present in memory
Speed up the execution of a program

explanation: Unable to execute injected code because of DEP, ROP reuses existing "gadgets" to build the exploit.

stem: Why is malware often "packed" with a packer such as UPX?

choices.text: To digitally sign itself with a certificate authority
To improve its network communication performance
To compress or encrypt its code and make static analysis harder
Solely to reduce its RAM consumption

explanation: Packing hides the real code until it is unpacked in memory, hindering disassembly and signature-based detection.

stem: How is exfiltrated data typically encoded in a DNS tunneling attack?

choices.text: In the HTTP User-Agent header of web requests
In the subdomains of queries sent to a domain controlled by the attacker
In the TTL field of the IP packets carrying the DNS traffic
In the EXIF metadata of uploaded images

explanation: The data is encoded in subdomain labels resolved by the attacker's authoritative name server.

stem: Which indicator most often gives away a DNS exfiltration tunnel?

choices.text: A complete absence of DNS queries on the network
Repeated outbound SSH connections
A sudden drop in outbound HTTPS traffic
An abnormally high volume of TXT queries or very long subdomains directed at a single domain

explanation: Long subdomains and a high query volume (often TXT) toward a single domain are classic markers.

stem: Why is DNS such a favored exfiltration channel for attackers?

choices.text: DNS guarantees ordered, reliable delivery like TCP
DNS traffic is rarely blocked and often lightly inspected on egress
DNS leaves absolutely no trace in any log
DNS natively encrypts all the data it carries

explanation: Almost always allowed outbound and rarely monitored, DNS offers a discreet channel for leaking data.

stem: Which technique does malware use to evade automated sandbox analysis?

choices.text: Signing itself with a valid developer certificate
Artificially inflating its file size
Detecting signs of a virtualized environment and staying dormant if it finds any
Opening a local listening port as soon as it starts

explanation: By spotting virtualization artifacts (drivers, MAC addresses, low activity), the malware suspends its malicious behavior.

stem: What is a time-based sandbox evasion?

choices.text: Sending mass spam to saturate the network
Immediately opening a backdoor at startup
Delaying malicious execution (long sleep, waiting for user interaction) to outlast the analysis window
Encrypting the victim's disk without any delay

explanation: By deferring its activity beyond the sandbox's observation window, the malware appears harmless during analysis.

stem: Which type of artifact can only be obtained through RAM analysis, and not through a cold disk examination?

choices.text: Prefetch files stored in the Windows folder
Cleartext encryption keys and injected processes that exist only in RAM
The disk partition table
System log files written to disk

explanation: Decrypted keys, code injections, and transient data exist only in memory and vanish when the machine is powered off.

stem: What is the Volatility framework mainly used for in digital forensics?

choices.text: Cracking passwords by brute force
Physically cloning a hard drive sector by sector
Analyzing a RAM dump to extract processes, connections, and artifacts
Scanning a network for live hosts

explanation: Volatility parses a memory image to reconstruct the process list, network connections, and other volatile artifacts.

stem: Why is a write blocker used when acquiring an evidence drive?

choices.text: To automatically encrypt the resulting copy
To compress the disk image on the fly
To prevent any modification of the source media and preserve the integrity of the evidence
To speed up the data copy

explanation: A write blocker allows reads but forbids writes to the original drive, guaranteeing that it remains unaltered.

stem: Why is a hash computed both before AND after acquiring a disk image?

choices.text: To compress the image and save space
To automatically delete temporary files
To index the copied files faster
To prove that the copy is identical to the original and was not altered during acquisition

explanation: Two matching hashes attest that the image is a faithful, unaltered copy of the source media.

stem: According to the order of volatility (RFC 3227), which of these items must be collected BEFORE the contents of the hard drive?

choices.text: The ARP table and active network connections
The contents of a backup CD-ROM
Log files archived on magnetic tape
The physical configuration and topology of the server

explanation: The ARP table and the state of network connections are highly volatile and disappear when the machine is powered off, so they are collected before the far more stable disk.

stem: What is an attacker trying to achieve by performing "timestomping" on a compromised system?

choices.text: Altering a file's MACB timestamps to mislead timeline analysis
Encrypting the metadata of the NTFS partition
Improving file system performance
Synchronizing the server clock with an NTP server

explanation: Timestomping falsifies a file's dates (modified, accessed, created) to muddle the chronological reconstruction of the attack.

stem: Why should a memory dump not be written to the local disk of the machine being analyzed during a RAM capture?

choices.text: RAM technically cannot be copied to a disk
Windows forbids all disk writes during a memory acquisition
It overwrites unallocated space and can destroy artifacts present on the disk
The local disk is always encrypted and refuses writes
