explanation: Writing to the source disk alters unallocated space and potentially destroys evidence, hence the use of external media.

stem: Which element is essential right from collection to guarantee the integrity of digital evidence within the chain of custody?

choices.text: A cryptographic hash computed at the time of collection
The signature of the installed antivirus vendor
The session password of the suspect user
A printed copy of the file's contents

explanation: The hash computed at collection time makes it possible to prove later that the evidence has not been modified.

stem: What can analyzing the .pf files in the Windows Prefetch folder primarily establish?

choices.text: The full contents of the files opened by the user
The passwords typed on the keyboard during the session
That an executable was run, when, and how many times
The complete list of websites visited

explanation: Prefetch keeps a record of a binary's executions: the last run time and a run counter.

stem: In which registry hive is the "Run" key that provides startup persistence specific to the profile of the logged-on user?

choices.text: HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT
HKEY_CURRENT_CONFIG
HKEY_CURRENT_USER
HKEY_PERFORMANCE_DATA

explanation: The ...\CurrentVersion\Run key under HKEY_CURRENT_USER runs when that specific user logs on.

stem: Which Windows Security log Event ID corresponds to a successful logon?

choices.text: Event ID 4625
Event ID 1102
Event ID 7045
Event ID 4624

explanation: ID 4624 marks a successful logon, versus 4625 for a failure, 1102 for an audit log clear, and 7045 for a service installation.

stem: Which Windows artifact can prove that an executable existed on the system, even after it has been deleted?

choices.text: The Recycle Bin once emptied
The hosts file
The DNS resolver cache
The AppCompatCache (Shimcache) and the AmCache

explanation: Shimcache and AmCache retain references to executables the system has encountered, even ones since deleted from disk.

stem: A side-channel attack exploits…

choices.text: An open network port
A phishing email
Physical leakage (execution time, power consumption, emissions) rather than a logical flaw
A weak password

explanation: A secret (e.g. a key) is inferred by measuring the system's physical behavior, without breaking the algorithm itself.

stem: A ROP chain (Return-Oriented Programming) bypasses NX/DEP protection by:

choices.text: Leaking the base address of the stack in order to disable ASLR
Injecting shellcode directly onto the stack and then jumping to it
Rewriting the stack permissions through a canary overflow
Chaining "gadgets" (short sequences of existing code ending in a ret instruction) already present in executable pages

explanation: NX prevents injected code from executing, but ROP reuses already-executable code by chaining gadgets through the stack, thereby circumventing data non-executability.

stem: A format string vulnerability (e.g. printf(user_input)) notably allows an attacker:

choices.text: To read and write at arbitrary addresses through format specifiers (%x and %s to read, %n to write)
To bypass the HTML escaping of a web page
To perform a SQL injection in the formatted query
To cause a denial of service only, with no memory access whatsoever

explanation: An attacker-controlled format string reads the stack with %x/%s and writes the number of bytes output so far to a target address with %n, providing an arbitrary read/write primitive.

stem: A dependency confusion (supply chain) attack succeeds when:

choices.text: A malicious public package sharing the name of an internal package takes precedence during dependency resolution
An attacker modifies the SHA-256 hash pinned in the lockfile
A private registry rejects requests because of an expired token
A developer accidentally commits an API key to a public repository

explanation: By publishing to a public registry a package with the same name as an internal dependency (usually with a higher version number), the attacker gets their malicious version installed instead of the legitimate one.

stem: The POODLE attack exploits:

choices.text: The factorization of 512-bit "export" RSA keys
Nonce reuse in AES-GCM under TLS 1.2
The non-deterministic padding of SSL 3.0 in CBC mode, of which only the last byte is verified, combined with a forced downgrade to SSL 3.0
A flaw in the renegotiation mechanism specific to TLS 1.3

explanation: POODLE forces a fallback to SSL 3.0, whose CBC padding is not fully verified, turning the server into an oracle that reveals one byte of the secret at a time.

stem: Differential Power Analysis (DPA) recovers a secret key by:

choices.text: Injecting faults through voltage glitching to skip instructions
Statistically correlating the measured power consumption with the intermediate values predicted for each subkey hypothesis
Listening to the processor's acoustic emissions to reconstruct keystrokes
Measuring the total execution time of the encryption for each candidate key

explanation: DPA collects many power traces and exploits the correlation between measured power and a model of the intermediate values to distinguish the correct subkey hypothesis from noise.

stem: A timing attack against a naive implementation of RSA modular exponentiation (square-and-multiply) exploits:

choices.text: The fact that the conditional multiplication is performed only for the exponent bits set to 1, creating a timing variation correlated with the private key
PKCS#1 v1.5 padding verification performed in non-constant time
The predictability of the generator producing the primes p and q
The reuse of the same public exponent e across several distinct messages

explanation: A multiplication performed only when an exponent bit is 1 makes the computation time depend on the bits of the private key, which the attacker reconstructs through statistical measurements.

stem: Reusing a nonce with the same key in AES-GCM is catastrophic because it allows an attacker:

choices.text: To instantly recover the underlying 256-bit AES key
To invert the HMAC-SHA256 function used alongside it
To recover the GHASH authentication key H and therefore forge valid tags for arbitrary messages
To desynchronize the block counter, affecting confidentiality only

explanation: Two messages under the same nonce yield linear equations over GF(2^128) that reveal the authentication key H, destroying integrity and enabling tag forgery.

stem: The Spectre attack (variant 1, bounds check bypass) relies on:

choices.text: A buffer overflow in the motherboard's UEFI firmware
Speculative execution past a bounds check, whose traces persist in the cache and are read out through a side channel
Reading kernel memory made possible by a complete lack of address space isolation
A direct privilege escalation through an insufficiently filtered system call

explanation: The CPU speculatively performs an out-of-bounds access before rolling the operation back; the footprint left in the cache is then extracted with a Flush+Reload measurement.

stem: A "return-to-libc" attack bypasses a non-executable stack by:

choices.text: Exploiting a format string to call dlopen() remotely
Injecting encrypted shellcode that libc would decrypt at runtime
Redirecting the return address to a libc function (e.g. system()) with its arguments set up on the stack
Rewriting the GOT to point to code injected on the heap

explanation: Rather than executing injected code, the attacker diverts control flow to an already-present, executable libc function and supplies it with attacker-controlled arguments via the stack.

stem: Heap grooming (or heap feng shui) is used during exploitation to:

choices.text: Encrypt heap metadata to evade detection
Compress the heap to reduce the exploit's memory footprint
Detect the presence of a canary before triggering the overflow
Manipulate allocations and frees to deterministically place a controlled object at the targeted memory location

explanation: By orchestrating a sequence of allocations and frees, the attacker shapes the heap layout to make corruption of an adjacent target object reliable and predictable.

stem: A "padding oracle" attack exploits…

choices.text: An unchanged default password
A stored XSS injection
A system that reveals whether the padding of a decrypted message is valid, making decryption possible without the key
A simple buffer overflow

explanation: By observing "valid/invalid padding" responses (e.g. in CBC mode), the plaintext is reconstructed byte by byte.

stem: A double free vulnerability can lead to code execution because:

choices.text: It forces the kernel to remap the page as read-write-execute
It discloses the uninitialized contents of a reused chunk
The freelist is corrupted, so a later allocation can return a pointer to an attacker-controlled area
The second free() directly overwrites the return address on the stack

explanation: Freeing a chunk twice introduces an inconsistency in the list of free blocks that the attacker exploits to make malloc return a pointer into an area they control.

stem: An integer overflow in an allocation size computation (e.g. count × element_size) creates a memory vulnerability when:

choices.text: The compiler optimizes the multiplication into an incorrect bit shift
The CPU raises a hardware exception that aborts the exploit
The size, having become negative, merely causes an unchecked null pointer to be returned
The product exceeds the capacity of the integer type, allocating an undersized buffer that is then filled with all the elements, overflowing the heap

explanation: The overflow truncates the result modulo the size of the type, so the resulting allocation is smaller than intended and writing the elements overflows the buffer.

stem: Typosquatting in a package ecosystem (npm, PyPI) consists of:

choices.text: Injecting malicious code into the CI pipeline through a compromised runner
Replacing a package's integrity hash in the official registry
Publishing a malicious package under a name very close to a popular legitimate package, betting on developers' typos
Hijacking the legitimate maintainer's account through credential stuffing

explanation: Typosquatting bets on a mistyped name of a common dependency to get a look-alike package containing malicious code installed instead.

stem: The CRIME attack against TLS exploits:

choices.text: A forced downgrade to SSL 3.0 in CBC mode
The reuse of a predictable IV across successive records
An MD5 collision in the certificate signature
Compression applied before encryption: the ciphertext size varies with redundancy, revealing a secret placed next to chosen data

explanation: Since compression shrinks the message more the closer the secret resembles the attacker-injected data, the observed ciphertext length acts as an oracle for guessing that secret (e.g. a cookie).

stem: Bleichenbacher's attack (the "million message attack") against RSA targets:

choices.text: A nonce weakness in the associated ECDSA signature
A PKCS#1 v1.5 padding oracle that reveals whether a decrypted ciphertext starts with the bytes 0x00 0x02, allowing the message to be recovered through adaptive queries
The reuse of the same exponent e across coprime moduli (Håstad's attack)
Factoring the RSA modulus with Pollard's rho algorithm

explanation: By sending crafted ciphertexts and observing whether the server accepts the PKCS#1 v1.5 padding, the attacker progressively narrows the interval containing the plaintext until it is fully recovered.

stem: An "Encrypt-then-MAC" construction protects against padding oracle attacks because:

choices.text: It replaces CBC mode with ECB, which is immune to padding issues
It enforces non-deterministic random padding on every block
The MAC is verified over the ciphertext before any decryption, so a tampered ciphertext is rejected without ever revealing padding validity
It encrypts the MAC, preventing the attacker from reading it

explanation: By validating the authenticity of the ciphertext before decrypting it, any tampered message is discarded at MAC verification, so the padding oracle is never reached.

stem: The Meltdown attack differs from Spectre in that it:

choices.text: Exploits a hash collision in the page table
Reads kernel memory directly from user space by exploiting out-of-order execution before the permission exception is raised
Only works on processors that lack speculative execution
Requires training the victim's branch predictor beforehand

explanation: Meltdown takes advantage of the kernel data being read transiently before the permission check cancels the instruction, leaving an exploitable trace in the cache, without going through the branch predictor as Spectre does.

stem: A stack canary (stack cookie) can notably be bypassed by:

choices.text: Encrypting the shellcode so that it passes the cookie check
Disabling NX to make the stack executable
Leaking the canary value through a memory read, then rewriting it unchanged during the overflow
Overflowing by a single byte, which never reaches the canary

explanation: If a read vulnerability discloses the canary, the attacker writes it back verbatim in their overflow, so the check at the end of the function still passes.

stem: Address Space Layout Randomization (ASLR) is most often bypassed by:

choices.text: Exploiting an information leak (info leak) that reveals a runtime address, allowing a module's base address to be recomputed
Using only relative addresses in the injected shellcode
Reserving all of virtual memory until the randomization entropy is exhausted
Forcing the kernel to disable ASLR through a remote environment variable

explanation: A leaked address gives away a module's real position in memory; the attacker derives its base and recomputes every address (gadgets, functions) needed for exploitation.

stem: The compromise of the xz/liblzma library (2024) illustrates a supply chain attack in which:

choices.text: A malicious maintainer introduced a backdoor triggered at build time, hidden in test files and activated under specific conditions
An internal npm registry was confused with its public counterpart
A public repository was typosquatted to fool developers in a hurry
A GPG signing key was leaked in a public repository

explanation: A contributor who had earned the project's trust inserted a backdoor activated during the build, hidden in test data, aimed at compromising SSH authentication on systems linked against liblzma.

stem: The "Flush+Reload" cache attack allows an attacker to:

choices.text: Flush the TLB to force the victim to reload its pages from disk
Encrypt communications between two cores by exploiting the cache coherence protocol
Infer which cache lines a victim accessed by measuring the reload time of data previously evicted from a shared memory region
Modify the contents of a neighboring core's L1 cache to corrupt the victim's data

explanation: Flush+Reload evicts a shared line and then measures its reload latency: a fast access betrays that the victim reloaded it, revealing their memory accesses.

stem: The "Bellcore" fault injection attack against RSA-CRT recovers the private key because:

choices.text: The fault directly turns the private exponent d into its public inverse e
A fault during one of the two modular computations (mod p or mod q) produces a faulty signature whose GCD with the modulus reveals a prime factor
It forces the generator to reuse p and q values already known to the attacker
It triggers an overflow that exposes the key in cleartext in a processor register

explanation: Given one correct signature and one faulted through the same CRT computation, gcd(s - s', N) yields a prime factor of N, which completely breaks the RSA key.

stem: In a "padding oracle" attack against CBC mode, the attacker recovers the plaintext by:

choices.text: Forcing the server to reuse a nonce in order to align the keystreams
Manipulating the previous ciphertext block and observing whether the server reports valid padding after decryption
Guessing the symmetric key directly from the observed ciphertext blocks
Replaying the same IV until a block collision occurs

explanation: By modifying the previous block byte by byte and reading the padding oracle's reaction, the attacker deduces the decryption output, and therefore the plaintext, without knowing the key.

stem: Reusing the same nonce with the same key in a stream cipher or in CTR mode allows an attacker to:

choices.text: Trigger a counter overflow that exposes the IV in cleartext
Immediately recover the secret key through a reduced brute force
Invert the hash function used to derive the nonce
Obtain the XOR of the two plaintexts by combining the two ciphertexts, since the identical keystream cancels out

explanation: Two messages encrypted with the same keystream satisfy C1 XOR C2 = P1 XOR P2, which eliminates the keystream and leaks the relationship between the plaintexts.

stem: The fundamental weakness of the ECB encryption mode is that:

choices.text: Padding is systematically omitted, exposing the exact length of the message
Each block depends on the previous one, propagating any transmission error
It requires a predictable IV that can be replayed from one message to the next
Identical plaintext blocks produce identical ciphertext blocks, revealing structural patterns of the message

explanation: ECB encrypts each block independently with the same key, so repetitions in the plaintext remain visible in the ciphertext (the classic "ECB penguin" illustration).

stem: The BEAST attack against TLS 1.0 exploits the fact that:

choices.text: Certificates using 1024-bit RSA can be factored in real time during the handshake
TLS compression leaks the length of encrypted cookies
A forced downgrade imposes the use of the RC4 stream cipher
The IV of each CBC record is the last encrypted block of the previous one, hence predictable, which enables an adaptive chosen-plaintext attack

explanation: TLS 1.0 chains a record's IV to the last block of the previous one; this predictable IV lets BEAST test hypotheses byte by byte about a secret using chosen plaintexts.

stem: A heap overflow becomes exploitable for code execution notably when it:

choices.text: Exceeds the size of the .text segment mapped read-only
Corrupts the canary placed between the local variables and the return address
Fills the call stack until the return address is overwritten
Overwrites the metadata of a neighboring chunk (freelist pointers), which is then used during a later free() or malloc()

explanation: By overwriting the allocator's chunk management pointers, the attacker hijacks the freelist unlinking to obtain an arbitrary write during a subsequent memory operation.

stem: A use-after-free vulnerability is most often exploited by:

choices.text: Reading an uninitialized local variable to leak an address
Freeing the same pointer twice to corrupt the stack directly
Overflowing a static buffer located in the BSS segment
Reallocating the freed chunk's space with controlled data before the dangling pointer is dereferenced

explanation: The attacker forces a new allocation to reoccupy the freed region with their own data, so that use of the dangling pointer operates on an object they control.
